coredata.dc:title: Exploring two decades of graphene: The jack of all trades
coredata.dc:creator: Santra, Sumon
Bose, Avigyan
Mitra, Koushik
Adalder, Ashadul
coredata.dc:description: In the last two decades, graphene and its derivative structures have proved effective in various applications, having a significant impact on society. In all kinds of research field whether it is the energy, environmental, biomedical or any interdisciplinary field, graphene have proved effective. The modifiable physical and chemical properties of its derivative products have made way for advanced research. Here in this review, we have discussed the advent of graphene-based materials in research over the past 20 years. Research areas related to the fields of environmental, energy and electronic, and biomedical have been discussed. For environmental research pollution, purification, and analyte sensing aspects were considered. In energy research, green energy generation and energy storage materials based on graphene were discussed. Finally, in the biomedical sections, various biological applications of graphene were discussed. We have also tried to highlight the engineering aspect and synthesis pathways that led to the rise of the graphene empire in every domain of materials science and nanoscience research. The overall details in this review may help the reader, in general, to understand why graphene is designated as the jack of all trades in research.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the last two decades, graphene and its derivative structures have proved effective in various applications, having a significant impact on society. In all kinds of research field whether it is the energy, environmental, biomedical or any interdisciplinary field, graphene have proved effective. The modifiable physical and chemical properties of its derivative products have made way for advanced research. Here in this review, we have discussed the advent of graphene-based materials in research over the past 20 years. Research areas related to the fields of environmental, energy and electronic, and biomedical have been discussed. For environmental research pollution, purification, and analyte sensing aspects were considered. In energy research, green energy generation and energy storage materials based on graphene were discussed. Finally, in the biomedical sections, various biological applications of graphene were discussed. We have also tried to highlight the engineering aspect and synthesis pathways that led to the rise of the graphene empire in every domain of materials science and nanoscience research. The overall details in this review may help the reader, in general, to understand why graphene is designated as the jack of all trades in research.

Graphene and its derivative materials have proved to have used in multipurpose applications in the last two decades. Graphene-based materials and their engineering aspects contributed largely in the environment, energy, and biomedical fields and are still popular.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The present developing world relies in significant terms on advancements in the field of material science and nanotechnology. With the rising trend in the attainment of sustainability [1,2], there has been much focus on carbon-based nanomaterials [3,4]. The present requirement of the world is to have a pollution-free environment and reduce greenhouse gas emissions. Harvesting energy, providing alternatives to fossil fuels [5], and relying more on green energy [6]. Last but not least, it has a better biomedical infrastructure to provide large-scale medical support to the common mass at a low cost. Carbon-based nanomaterials have been an ideal choice over other similar metal-based systems to provide for the above-mentioned facilities. One common example of a non-carbon-based material having properties and applications similar to graphene is MoS2 [7,8]. Since graphene is totally carbon-based, it could be said that this material has more prospects in the sustainable circular economy in the upcoming future. Carbon-based nanomaterials like carbon nanotubes [9,10], fullerenes[11], and nanodiamonds [12] have proved extremely effective in this scenario.

One such carbon-based nanomaterial is ‘Graphene’ [13]. The famous quote by physicist Richard Phillips Feynman "There is Plenty of Room at the Bottom” was well established by the wonder material ‘Graphene’. The multifunctional properties of graphene and its derivatives have made it clear that it is a future material. In the last two decades of research, graphene has proven its capabilities in diverse fields like environmental, energy, and medical applications. Graphene is purely composed of carbon atoms, and its shape and surface could be tuned to the desired applications. Graphene can be modified with various functional groups, can be doped with different heteroatoms, and also has defects in its structure, which have led to its application in various sectors [14]. Besides having a versatile surface property owing to the above-mentioned facts, graphene has been widely used in the development of a composite system for enhanced performance [15,16].

Graphene is regarded as an allotrope of carbon. Here all the carbon atoms are bonded together and arranged in a hexagonal lattice arrangement. The layers between the graphene are bound together by Van der Waals forces [17]. The unusual band structure of graphene has classified it as a semi-metallic material and has an active electronic property. It is unusual compared to a conventional semiconductor since graphene has a band gap of zero[17]. The rich electron-conducting surface of graphene has made it convenient for use in applications requiring rich electron and charge transport[18,19]. Other than its electronic properties graphene has high mechanical strength. This mechanical strength makes it stand out compared to other materials. This mechanical support of graphene helps prevent the deformation and distortion of the fabricated product to a great extent. Graphene is a single layer of graphite. As 2D nanosheets graphene has other forms like graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO), and partially reduced graphene oxide. Whereas in 0D form graphene can be categorized into graphene quantum dots (GQD) [20] and graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQD) [17]. Each of these forms has shown interesting applications. The various applications of graphene include water remediation, air purification, desalination, catalysis, energy conversion, energy storage, solar cells, displays, drug delivery, biological equipment, Etc.

In this review, we have presented an overview and research update on the different categories of graphene-based materials and the use of the diverse applications of graphene in the fields of environmental remediation, energy and electronics, and biomedical in the last two decades. A schematic representation of the same is demonstrated in Fig. 1 . Also, the conclusion regarding how the implementation of this wonder material on an industrial scale can help in future advancement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Common graphitic structures and their synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: In graphene and its related structures, the carbon bonds can be arranged in different forms and have different surface functional groups. Based on the various forms they can be classified into six basic categories. They are the graphite structure where layers of single-layered graphene are stacked one over another in a 3D arrangement[21]. The single layer as said in graphite is known as graphene[13]. They can be monolayer or a few layers thick. The next category is graphene oxide which could be easily prepared through chemical methods by the oxidation of graphite flakes[22]. The reduced graphene oxide can be subsequently prepared by the reduction of the prepared graphene oxide[23]. The nano-sized (2–10 nm) quantum dots having graphitic carbon core are known as graphene quantum dots[24]. The graphene quantum dots that bear various other oxygen functional groups on their surface are known as graphene oxide quantum dots [24]. Similar structural representations of these structures have been represented in the Fig. 2 . In the case of graphene, graphene oxide, and reduced graphene oxide all have similar characteristics D and G bands in the raman spectra.

The wonder material graphene is a two-dimensional material. The stacked layer of graphene is known as graphite. Graphite is a well-known stable crystalline carbon form that could be naturally obtained. Graphite has a distinguished soft nature. One of the basic characteristics of graphite as a rock is that it creates a black mark when it is scratched on the surface of a paper, and has a distinct dull black colour[21]. It is the graphite that under extreme temperature, and pressure conditions can be converted to the rare carbon form (Diamond). In extreme conditions, the sp2 hybridized form in graphite is converted to sp3 in the diamond. In the molecular structure of graphite, the carbon atoms are arranged in a hexagonal format having stacked with each layer in the AB sequence[21]. Each layer of graphite has a honeycomb lattice structure. Here the bond length between each carbon atom in each layer's honeycomb lattice is 0.142 nm and that between the planes is 0.335 nm[21].

Pristine Graphene, or simply monolayer graphene, is an isolated single layer of graphite that consists of only carbon atoms. So, in general, graphene can be called the basic fundamental structure of graphite[13]. Graphene, due to the presence of rich sp2 bonds has high electronic and mechanical properties. Due to this excellent physiochemical behaviour, they are widely used in various applications. However, one of the biggest disadvantages of graphene is its synthesis. Being one single-atom thin, the purity and defect-less structure are highly compromised during synthesis. Even if graphene oxide is reduced to reduced graphene oxide, the electronic performance is not up to comparison with that of pristine graphene[25]. Graphene, being highly reactive, could be tuned in various reactions. One of the simplest examples is when they are used for hydrogenation purposes for the generation of graphene [26]. In comparison to graphene, which is semi-metallic in nature, graphane is an insulator with significantly lowered strain[27].

Synthesis of pristine graphene is a matter of challenge to the researchers as it is one of the most purified forms of graphene without any type of contamination. Some of the procedures that can be employed for the synthesis of pristine graphene include mechanical exfoliation, chemical vapor deposition, chemical synthesis methods, etc. The process of mechanical exfoliation is one of the most primitive techniques for the synthesis of few-layered graphene. The process was first reported by K. S. Novoselov and A. K. Geim in 2004 which gave them a noble prize in 2010. The process includes the repeated peeling of highly oriented pyrolytic graphite by the use of scotch tape[13]. The process although allowing to fabricate pristine few-layered graphene is not industrially used for upscaling production of graphene, since the yield is low. Other synthesis techniques like chemical vapor deposition offer high-quality synthesis of graphene [28]. A work by Li et al. in 2009 demonstrated that graphene can be grown on a large area, to obtain graphene sheets in the order of centimetres[29]. The main importance of growing large-sized graphene nanosheets is to enhance their electronic performance. Chemical synthetic procedures as a top-down approach have been often undertaken by groups of scientists, which has also given them satisfactory yields. Small-scale approaches for the synthesis of graphene nanoflakes had been undertaken by Kumar et al. [8], where they had employed sonication of graphite in SDS solution at room temperature for 6 h as their optimized modus operandi. The supernatant was collected after letting the solution stand, followed by centrifugation to eliminate the larger undesired products like the unstable or larger flakes. The role of SDS as a surfactant in these kinds of synthetic procedures were also investigated [30] and it was found that the surfactant helps in dispersing the precursors in the solvent, while sonication or stirring provides the sufficient impetus to break the van der Waals bonds that construct the graphitic structure. Commercially available expandable graphite, which consists of bisulphate ions intercalated between layers [31], has also been used as a source of pristine graphene. Exfoliation of the material by heating in Ar atmosphere, followed by sonicating in a solution of N-methyl pyrrolidone and collecting the supernatant, has yielded pristine graphene sheets suitable for the production of other nanostructures such as pristine graphene/metal-nanoparticle hybrids [32].

Graphene oxide could be easily described as the graphene nanosheet having various oxygen-containing functional groups. This commonly includes carboxylic (–COOH), hydroxyl (–OH), alkoxy (C–O–C), and carbonyl (C = O) functional groups. The various oxygen-containing functional groups in it have various exceptional properties which make it an extremely useful material. Some of its exceptional properties include high mechanical, thermal, electrical, and surface functional properties, which are similar to those of graphene. When talking about the structure they are graphene sheets where various oxygen functional groups are arranged randomly. It has been commonly observed that the hydroxyl and epoxy groups are randomly distributed on the surface of the graphene oxide while the carbonyl and carboxylic groups are mostly centred on the edge sites of those compounds[33]. Although the chemical properties of the functional groups on the surface are dependent on the pH the carboxylic groups on the edges are generally charged in nature while the polar groups present on the basal plane are uncharged[33]. Being charged, high carboxylic groups content in graphene can act as good high-energy electrodes. In a work by Obraztsov et al., they found that graphene acid, a graphene derivative with high carboxylic group content, can act as a good material for high-performance lithium-ion batteries[34]. Unlike the structure of graphene whose surface is hydrophobic in nature, due to the presence of various polar functional groups on graphene oxide, it is hydrophilic in nature. Due to this hydrophilic nature, they are very easily dispersed in water. But graphene oxide can also be made hydrophobic by modifying the functional groups, then they could be dispersed in other organic solvents[35]. Due to the abundance of different functional groups graphene oxide could be easily functionalized. Because of this easy functional tunability, they are widely used in various applications. The various applications are described in the following sections.

The most commonly used synthesis method for graphene oxide is the Hummers method [36,37]. There have been several modifications in the reagent which have ultimately led to the Modified Hummers method[38]. This method can be optimized as per the requirement. One commonly used schematic representation of the synthesis procedure for GO is given below in Fig. 3(a) . During synthesis, raw graphite powder is taken in a round bottom flask or beaker. Additionally, sodium nitrate could be added to the graphite powder. Sodium in sodium nitrate helps increase the interlayer distance between the graphene sheets by intercalating in between them. It has also been observed that with the implementation of NaNO3, there has been a significant increase in the oxidation of the basal planes of the graphene sheets. This results in strain induction between the sheets and sheets get cracked along the a-axis and are peeled off along the c-axis. As a result of the crack along the a-axis the GO produced with NaNO3 has a smaller crystallite size. Now this depends on the researcher's necessity and the required application. If in any certain application where larger GO crystals are required, then NaNO3 should not be used[39]. NaNO3 could be ball milled with graphite powder. If low mesh-size ball-milling is not available simple mortar pestle could be used for grinding. The next choice is the solvent ratio selection. An extremely harsh and acidic environment is used for GO synthesis. Mainly a mixture of conc. H2SO4 and conc. H3PO4 is used. Both of them have different roles. H3PO4 is used for exfoliating the layers of graphite where as H2SO4 is used for introducing the oxygen-rich functionalities[40]. In general, a ratio of 9:1 (by volume) of conc. H2SO4 and conc. H3PO4 is used and resulted in increasing the yield of GO production and simultaneously decreasing the amount of production of toxic gases[38]. The mixture is taken in a round bottom flask/beaker and is stirred under an ice bath. The ice bath is used to prevent exothermic reactions from taking place after KMnO4 addition, as this will prevent the effective formation of oxygen-rich functional groups. The next step is the addition of KMnO4. This is taken in a 6:1 ratio (by weight) compared to graphite flakes. The KMnO4 taken is thoroughly ground to a fine powder. Some of the other techniques for the synthesis of graphene oxide include the Staudenmaier method, Brodie method, and Hofmann method. The schematic representation of the synthesis process by the above-mentioned methods is shown in the Fig. 3(b).

The oxidized form of graphene is known as graphene oxide, similarly, the reduced form is known as reduced graphene oxide (rGO). One of the simplest approaches for the synthesis of rGO is the help of the chemical reduction of GO [42,43]. But it was found that even after the reduction of the rGO a few percentages of oxygen functional groups are present. But after the reduction of graphene oxide, the pi bond conjugation is highly increased which can be identified by the redshift of the UV-absorbance spectra. So here in rGO, the electron transport phenomenon occurs by the hopping mechanism[44]. Due to the presence of a large volume of pi-electron conjugation, they are widely used in energy storage applications [45].

The simplest method for the preparation of rGO is by the subsequent reduction of the graphene oxide. The synthesis procedure given below in Fig. 4 was given by a comparative study by Luo et al. These are the commonly used synthesis procedure for the preparation of rGO[46]. The two-step procedure among the six reduction strategies studied seems to be the best one for improving rGO synthesis according to this assessment criterion. In the study it was found that the two-step approach of rGO reduction improves electrical conductivity, defect repair degree, and reduction degree in the material[46].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nano-sized graphene fragments having a diameter of less than 20 nm. GQDs are known to possess infinite exciton Bohr diameter[47]. Similar to the semiconductor quantum dots, GQDs are highly fluorescent and responsive to excitation. The band gap of the graphene quantum dots has high tunability and this can be modified by changing the size and surface functional groups or by the insertion of dopants. GQDs can be easily doped with various dopants such as nitrogen, sulfur, chlorine, etc. In an ideal case, the DFT calculations on GQDs having 20 aromatic rings have been found to have a band gap of 2 eV whereas the band gap of benzene is found to be 7eV[48]. Graphene quantum dots have exceptional optoelectronic properties and also have been used in various energy storage applications due to their electronic structure. Their photoluminescence properties have also been utilized in various sensing, analytical, and biomedical applications. Due to their inertness and non-toxic behaviour, they have flourished in the biological field[49]. Both carbon quantum dots and graphene quantum dots consist of carbon core [50,51]. However, the basic difference between them is the presence of the graphitic core in the GQDs. The graphitic core is a characteristic proof of the GQDs. This could be easily proved by the use of an HR-TEM image, which could be used to see the graphitic structure of the GQDs. In most cases, the traditionally prepared GQDs emit blue to green emission wavelength on excitation. But in rare cases, GQDs have been reported to emit red fluorescence emission [52,53,54].

There are several common methods for the synthesis of graphene quantum dots. This includes chemical exfoliation methods, hydrothermal methods, solvothermal methods, electrochemical approaches, microwave synthesis, chemical vapor deposition Etc. In the hydrothermal method, the graphene sheets are cut inside the high-temperature autoclave reactor in the water medium. In a solvothermal reaction, any other organic solvent is taken instead of water for the preparation of the GQDs. The basic chemistry of this method is that it cuts the bonds within the bulk structure, depending upon the type of the same, under the conditions of high temperature and pressure. But firstly, the material has to undergo oxidation, often achieved by using strong oxidising agents such as sulphuric and nitric acids [55]. Even so, there are methods which employ acid-free conditions to make this possible. To cite an instance, Lee et al. [56] used a variety of carbon sources such as graphite (G), multiwall carbon nanotubes (M), carbon fibers (CF) and charcoal (C) to make G-GQDs, M-GQDs, CF-GQDs and C-GQDs respectively via acid-free synthesis method. Oxidants are essential in this process, and therefore they have used oxone with DMF as the solvent. The one-pot solvothermal synthesis included autoclaving the carbon source-oxone-DMF solution at 200 °C for 10 h. The oxone provided the free radicals such as hydroxyl and sulfate, which in turn facilitated the scission of CC bonds. The overall process is a combination of oxidation via radicals and solvothermal reduction, also termed as solvothermal redox reaction. The QDs were separated from the insufficiently reacted starting materials by passing the resulting solution through a nanoporous membrane and via subsequent dialysis. Recycling of the recollected starting materials was also done, thereby making it a productive synthesis scheme. In ultrasonic methods, ultrasonic sounds are used to cause breakage in the graphene sheets ultimately leading to GQDs[57]. One of the very important processes in the quantum dot synthesis process is its purification. Generally, a 0.22um membrane filter is used for the separation of the larger particles from the QDs while dialysis is performed for the separation of the unreacted molecular species. Performing both steps can help in the purification of the QDs. Microwave assisted pyrolysis of carbon sources like Aspartic acid in the presence of aqueous ammonium bicarbonate has been shown to yield GQDs too [58]. GQDs have been shown to be grown on hexagonal boron nitride surface [59] by atmospheric pressure chemical vapour deposition with tuneable thickness by adjusting the ratio of CH4:H2:Ar gases and thickness was determined by repeated AFM measurements.

Similar to graphene quantum dots, graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) have a graphitic structure. They are very rich in oxygen functional groups. Various works have been done to explore the easy synthesis of these materials. A work by Liu et al. 2017 showed a facile one-step process for the synthesis of GOQDs from graphite with an intermediate step of GO formation[60]. GOQDs owing to various oxygen surface functional groups have proved effective in various applications. The presence of oxygen rich functional groups aids it in easy dispersion in aqueous solutions. It has been found that the band gap of GOQDs is greater compared to that of the GQDs.

The raw graphite source can itself be used for the preparation of both GQDs and GOQDs by the liquid phase exfoliation methods. A work by Liu et al. showed that graphite could be used as a raw precursor for exfoliation in a different medium. When exfoliation was performed by the use of an organic solvent nanoscale graphene fragments (GQDs) were formed whereas when exfoliation was done following the Hummers method, GOQDs were produced[24]. The Fig. 5 represents the synthesis pathways of the different QDs depending on the path taken. By following the Hummers method, a large quantity of oxygen functional groups was produced. Similar to the above synthesis in a work by Ahirwar et al. demonstrated that GQDs as well as GOQDs can be successfully prepared from the electrochemical exfoliation of graphite when used as an electrode. It was found that by varying the NaOH concentration can itself alter the content of the oxygen functional groups. As a result of this formation of QDs with varying functional groups takes place. The two-deciding factor for this difference in the synthesis is the reaction temperature conditions and intercalation of the OH− ions[61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Other graphene-based derivatives on the type of bonds present
ce:section.ce:para: Other than classifying the graphitic structures based on their sizes and functional groups present, they can also be classified based on the type of bond present in them. They are mainly graphane[62], graphene, and graphyne[63]. Like we classify hydrocarbons in organic chemistry on the basis of type bonds, here graphane is predominantly composed of single bonds, graphene with double bonds, and graphyne with triple bonded acetylene linkage. As already said in the previous sections, that graphane was first prepared by the hydrogenation of graphene. But practically it is difficult to perform complete hydrogenation of graphene. As a general terminology, it can be said that a graphane is a hydrogenated form of graphene, but complete hydrogenation of graphene is very difficult. With the increase in the content of hydrogenation, the band gap of the material gradually increases. Graphane generally has a very large band gap compared to graphene. Similar to graphene, graphane has a regular hexagonal lattice arrangement. Here each carbon atom is bonded to two hydrogen atoms and two carbon atoms, forming a saturated sp3 hybridized structure[62]. These modifications alter the properties of graphene significantly. Due to the presence of a totally saturated system and large band gap, graphane is unable to act as a conductive support and hence does not allow the flow of electrons. While it acts as a good storage material for hydrogen. It has the potential to be used for the applications like hydrogen gas storage, dielectrics, coatings, etc.

Much like graphane, we have materials like fluorographene, which is basically the fluorinated form of graphene, one of the synthesizing methods of which is via the exfoliation (which includes liquid phase exfoliation using suitable solvents as well as mechanical exfoliation methods) of multi-layered fluorographite [64]. The comparison with graphane stems from the fact that there are also a significant proportion of sp3 hybridised C atoms in fluorographene, enabling conformations like boat and chair, possible as in graphane. Exfoliation is not the sole method of fluorographene synthesis since it can also be prepared by fluorination of individual graphene layers [64]. The structure is such that fluorine atoms bond with the graphene layers from both the top and the bottom[64]. The extent of fluorination can be tuned by tuning the reaction conditions, as indicated by Mazánek et al. The sp2/sp3 ratio of C atoms is also tunable, proving the fact that the material in itself is flexible with regrds to its chemical makeup, thereby causing an impact upon its properties for various applications.

Graphyne is another allotrope of carbon and it can be described as an extended form of graphene due to the presence of acetylene groups in between the graphene structures. More specifically benzene rings are interconnected by acetylene linkage to form a network structure[63]. Unlike graphane and graphene it is composed of sp and sp2 hybridized systems. The hybridization is dependent on the acetylene groups. Graphyne exists in different structural forms, this includes α-graphyne and β-graphyne[63]. Due to the presence of conjugated systems like that of graphene it could be used in the applications like in semiconductors, energy storage, catalysis, gas separation, etc.
If summed up in total the various types of graphene-based derivatives could be schematically represented in the following order as showed in the Fig. 6 .
Other than the wide varieties of graphene-based derivatives as discussed in the above sections, the engineering aspects of the material designing is equally important.

The most common thing that is performed in all categories of graphitic structures for modification in its chemical and physical properties is doping. The doping by the use of heteroatoms helps in the modification of the electronic structure. Depending on the types of dopants used, they are further classified as n-type or p-type doping. When there is a change in the electronic properties it as overall affects the bandgap, Fermi energy level, electronic states, charge transport, electron conduction, Etc. Depending on the type of dopant used the bonding in the graphitic structure also changes. The Fig. 7(a) shown demonstrates the various bonding configurations in graphitic structures, due to the presence of various dopants[66]. The dopants can be added in two different paths by the graphitic structures either by the replacement of carbon atoms by the heteroatoms. Or by the use adsorption of the dopants on the graphitic layers[67]. Of the various different dopant atoms nitrogen is most widely studied. It has been found that graphitic nitrogen can give rise to n type semi conducting graphene[68]. Whereas pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen can give rise to either weak n-type or p-type behaviour[69]. In a recent work by Chae et al. demonstrated that p-type doping can be achieved in graphene with the aid of cationic nitrogen having superior electrical conductivity, and it is mainly due to the preserved planar structure of the graphene layers[70]. N-doped graphitic structures have various advantages in energy applications due to various factors such as high charge retention, capacitance, and increased charging/discharging states[67]. In GQDs, nitrogen doping was found to increase its luminescent properties[71]. Another very commonly used atom for the generation of electron-accepting (p-type doping) in a graphene system is the use of boron. In various materials boron has been commonly used for the generation of p-type doping. It can be seen in the Fig. 7(a) that boron leads to the formation of three covalent bonds. Doping boron significantly helps in modulating the band gap of the material. In a work by Tang et al. in 2012 it was found that doping boron in graphene leads to the change in the band gap from 0 to 0.54 eV with high p-type conductivity[72]. In recent studies B-doped graphene has also proven to have enhanced capacitance [73]. When speaking of the n-type doping, phosphorous has been found to be a good n-type dopant. Doping phosphorous in the graphitic systems has helped in several applications like ORR[74], NRR[75], and lithium-sulfur batteries[76]. Other than this there are several other atoms shown in the Fig. 7(a) which can effectively cause modifications in the band structure of graphene as per the requirements. Some of the common synthesis methods for causing doping in graphitic materials include chemical vapor deposition (CVD), ball milling, thermal annealing, wet chemical methods, etc. [77].

Where in most of the cases doping is done to modify the electronic properties of graphitic materials. This can be done either by various methods like surface transfer doping, substitutional doping, etc. [81]. But the question remains that how much can we modify the electronic properties of a graphene-based material by doping? The concept of synergistic effects has provoked for the implementation of multiple heteroatoms for the use as a dopant. And it was found that more or less every non-carbon atom used as a dopant did modify the electronic properties of graphene. Even birds’ droppings have been found to modify the catalytic performance of graphene[82,83]. The questions of maximum electronic modification of graphene-based materials still remains unanswered and requires more works.

Other than doping another important process for the modifications in the graphitic structures for various applications is the functionalization[84]. The basic difference between doping and functionalization is that in doping the dopants (atoms) are added to the lattice of the original material whereas in functionalization functional groups (molecules) are added for modifying the surface structure of the material. Functionalization can be done on the graphitic structures in two methods, either by covalent modifications or by noncovalent modifications. The covalent modifications can be subdivided into Nucleophilic substitution reaction, Electrophilic substitution reaction, Condensation reaction, and Addition reaction. In the case of covalent functionalization, multiple positions of surface graphene hybridization are changed from sp2 to sp3 which results in the significant loss of the electronic conjugation[85]. There are both pros and cons to the functionalization process. The functionalization of the surface can facilitate research areas based on sensing applications[86,87], drug delivery[88], pollutants adsorptions[89], energy applications [90], etc. They are mainly helpful in GO, GQDs, and GOQDs where functional groups are required for specific applications. But functionalization process is not helpful in the areas where high conductivity and charge transfer process is required for the application perspective. Since with the increase in the degree of the functionalization the electronic properties are gradually reduced as the conjugation of the graphene sheets is lost. Once the electronic conjugation is lost the electrical conductivity of the material decreases. It has been found both in the case of covalent and noncovalent functionalization the electrical conductivity is decreased. So here they are not helpful in applications like batteries, catalysis, capacitors, etc.

A defect in a crystal is an inherent property. From the Gibbs-Helmholtz relationship, it is well established that defects in a material can give rise to a more stable configuration. The point when the entropy factor (ΔS) dominates it leads to the increase in the number of available microstates and decreases the overall Gibbs free energy (ΔG) of the system up to a certain extent. What was once believed that 2D materials are unstable, is confirmed that they are stable despite the presence of various types of defects[91]. Graphitic materials are no exceptions to such defects. What is known about the typical pristine structure of graphene structure is not obtained during the fabrication process. Various types of defects are introduced here. The common types of defects that are found in the graphitic structures are represented in the Fig. 7(b). The introduction of the defects in the lattice is one of the prime methods for modifications of the fundamental properties of a material. Presence of defective sites modifies the localised electronic states which can help in applications like surface functionalization, catalytic sites, etc.

There are mainly three subcategories of the types of defects, point defects, line defects, and inner layer defects[78]. Localized disruptions or abnormalities at certain locations inside a crystal lattice are referred to as point defects. These flaws are caused by altered or missing individual atoms or ions. They could be substitutions of one or more carbon atoms in the lattice with any other atoms (like in the case of doping). In adatom defects, placing a carbon atom in the interstitial position is impossible due to extensive lattice straining. This defect can occur only when the excess atom is added to a third dimension in a two-dimensional graphene system. The excess carbon atom can be placed in one of the three positions, above the centre between two carbon-carbon bonds, directly above a carbon atom in a graphene lattice, and above in between the hexagonal carbon ring. Of all possible combinations, the first condition where the carbon atom is placed above the centre between two carbon-carbon bonds is energetically the most stable system[92]. When a single atom or multiple carbon atoms is absent from a perfect graphene lattice structure of graphene vacant sites or holes are generated. This leads to the formation of vacancies. There could be single vacancies, double vacancies, or multiple vacancies. When a carbon atom is absent from the perfect symmetry of the graphitic systems it gives rise to localized electronic states, affecting the optical and electronic properties of graphene. Not only this, but the induced strain in the lattice caused by the lack of atoms also affects mechanical qualities like stiffness and sensitivity to deformation. Another important kind of defect that is found in graphene is the Stone-Wales defect where there is an in-plane rotation of the carbon-carbon bonds at 90 ° There is no loss of the carbon atoms. This rotation leads to the degeneration of the conventional hexagonal lattice of the graphene structure and the formation of adjacent heptagon and pentagon rings takes place[93]. The Stone-Wales defect is an energetically unfavourable condition and forces the carbon atoms in the graphitic plane to go out of the plane. The tendency of carbon atoms to go out of plane due to Stone-Wales defect increases the tendency of the graphitic structure to take the shape of other morphological structures like carbon nanotubes and fullerenes.

Extended flaws that develop along the lines or planes of the crystal lattice are referred to as line defects or dislocations. In them, atoms or ions are bent or misaligned in a certain direction. Other than these atomic defects there could be dislocations. In graphitic structures, only edge dislocations are common. Edge dislocations can give rise to misalignment of the graphitic lattice. It is well known that defects and dislocations can migrate in a graphene lattice. The edge dislocation migration can occur by either gliding or climbing. And it was found that the dislocations tend to get combined through migration and might result in the annihilation of the structure[94]. Dislocations may to some extent improve the electronic properties of the graphene, but one major disadvantage is that it could result in a decrease in the mechanical properties of the material. In materials having layered structures, such as graphene or specific kinds of crystals, inner layer defects, also known as stacking faults, can appear. These flaws entail the displacement or misalignment of whole crystallographic layers. Errors during crystal formation or external factors can result in stacking defects. Some of the common stacking configurations in graphene are, perfect stackings, Bernal stackings, intrinsic stackings, and extrinsic stackings. No defect is present in perfect stacking, where each carbon atoms is directly placed over each other in a regular arrangement. Bernal defect occurs when one layer of graphene is above another in such a configuration such that the carbon atoms of one layer are above the hexagon formed by the six carbon atoms of the next layer. This stacking arrangement is also called AB stacking. It has been reported that having Bernal defects results in higher electron mobility[95]. In intrinsic stacking there is misalignment of the perfect hexagonal lattice. While in extrinsic stacking there is an inclusion of a foreign atom in between the graphene layers which results in the misalignment of the lattice arrangements. Interlayer interstitial occurs in graphene when a foreign atom is present. Interlayer interstitial can be introduced by doping, or by the chemical or physical intercalation methods. Sometime intercalations are done for effective separation of the graphene layers from the graphene sheets like in the case of use of NaNO3 in the graphene oxide synthesis by the modified Hummers method[38].

Engineering for the modification in the graphitic structures is not limited to the generation of doping, functionalization, and defects. Currently a variety of research has been conducted for the preparation of porous graphene structure both 2D and 3D. The 2D porous structure is mainly holey graphene and. The 3D porous structure includes graphene foam, graphene sponge, graphene hydrogel, graphene aerogel, graphene laminates, Etc. As the name suggests in holey graphene, perforation is done in the basal planes of the graphene sheets[41]. The holes present are in the nanometre to angstrom size range. The presence of pores facilitates more exposed edge surface and higher volume of active sites. While characterizing these materials, the holes present can be easily viewed under the transmission electron microscopes as shown in the Fig. 7(c) [96]. In case of 3D structures also, one thing is common, that is high surface area. Whether it is a graphene foam, sponge or aerogel, all this material has a high surface area, and could be experimentally proved from BET surface analysis[97]. The engineering aspect is to incorporate application specific holes in the graphene sheet. As discussed earlier graphene-based materials fabricated in a lab scale always contains some quantity of defects. Like in case of graphene nanomesh, which have a high quantity of nano sized pores[98]. They had a direct band gap with high surface area and porous structure, which helped them a lot for the applications as a gas separation membrane [99,100]. Due to the presence of porous networks, these materials have very light weight without compromising their mechanical properties. In a recent work by Myung et al. derived a light weight graphene aerogel having multifunctional properties. The prepared graphene aerogel has been represented in the Fig. 7(d). These categories of materials can be utilized in both environmental and energy applications. The graphene aerogel prepared in this work demonstrated high performance as coin cell type supercapacitors and also demonstrated high adsorption capacity for the removal of harmful organic dyes[80].

A single layer of hexagonally organized carbon atoms is known as pristine graphene. It has unique features that make it a highly sought-after material for many different purposes. Rigid characterization is necessary to comprehend and utilize the promise of pure graphene to the fullest extent possible. An overview of the characterization methods frequently used to look into the structural, electrical, and morphological characteristics of pristine graphene is given in this portion. AFM (Atomic Force Microscopy) is an effective method for determining the thickness and surface texture of unprocessed graphene. The atomic-scale features and imperfections of graphene may be seen in high-resolution pictures produced by AFM by scanning a pointed probe across the material's surface. Scanning tunneling microscopy (STM), an AFM-based approach, also offers insights into the electrical characteristics of pure graphene, allowing for the visualization of individual atoms and the determination of the local density of states by Li et al. (Fig. 8 (a-f)[101]. One of the most used methods for characterizing graphene is Raman spectroscopy. The number of graphene layers, degree of lattice disorder, and presence of doping may all be determined from the distinctive Raman spectra of graphene, which include the G and 2D peaks (Fig. 8g). The 2D peak is connected to the second-order scattering process and is dependent on the layer number, while the G peak denotes the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon atoms. The quality and structural integrity of pure graphene has been thoroughly examined using Raman spectroscopy by Ferrari et al. [102].. A method that is frequently used to examine the Fermi surface and electrical band structure of pure graphene is angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES). ARPES permits the measurement of the electronic dispersion relation as well as the detection of band gaps, Dirac cones, and other electronic characteristics by examining the energy and momentum of photoemitted electrons. The distinctive electrical characteristics of graphene, such as the linear energy-momentum dispersion of Dirac fermions, have been well supported by ARPES measurements (Bostwick et al., Fig. 8h[103].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO)
ce:section.ce:para: Due to their unique properties and potential uses, graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) are two notable derivatives of graphene that have attracted much attention. Understanding the structure, functional groups, and properties of these materials depends on accurate characterization. An overview of frequently used characterization methods to examine graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide is given in this section.

A popular method for determining the crystal structure, interlayer spacing, and layering of graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) materials are X-ray diffraction (XRD). The stacking configuration and structural changes in the optimization process are better understood using XRD. In GO, the interlayer spacing between GO sheets is often represented as a large and strong peak at a low angle (2θ) in XRD patterns (Fig. 8 i). Because of the existence of functional groups containing oxygen and water molecule interconnections, the basal spacing observed in the GO XRD pattern is much larger than that of pure graphene. The disordered and multilayered structure of GO was shown by peak broadening in the XRD pattern. The XRD patterns change significantly as GO transforms into rGO. The return of distinct graphene-like structures results from the removal of oxygen-containing functional groups and restoration of the sp2 carbon network during the reduction process. The development of new diffraction peaks or the increase in peak intensity corresponding to the (002) plane, which indicates the reduction of interlayer spacing and the recovery of a more ordered structure, serve as indicators of GO reduction. The degree of reduction and recovery of sp2 carbon domains can be determined by XRD examination of rGO [104]. It is common practice to measure graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide using Raman spectroscopy. The D, G, and D' bands, as well as other unique peaks, are often seen in the Raman spectra of GO. While the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon atoms is represented by the G band, their breathing mode is represented by the D band. Carbon atoms with SP3 spacing are believed to be responsible for the D' band. The intensity ratio between the D and G bands, which can give insight into the extent of reduction and structural changes, makes Raman spectroscopy an efficient tool to track the GO to rGO transformation[102]. The chemical composition and functional groups of graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide can be examined using a powerful technique known as X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 8j). The relative abundance of elements such as carbon, oxygen, and other elements can be assessed using XPS. Furthermore, it provides important details about the oxidation degree of carbon and the presence of functional groups including hydroxyl, epoxy, and carboxyl groups[105]. The thermal stability and weight loss behavior of graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide are investigated using thermo gravimetric analysis (TGA) (Fig. 8k). Both the degree of oxidation and the effectiveness of the reduction process can be determined using TGA's ability to measure weight loss as a function of temperature[106]. Graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide shapes and structures are often visualized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The high-resolution imaging made possible by TEM enables the examination of individual sheets, defects, and the organization of layers (Fig. 8l). The distribution, sheet size, and general shape of the materials are all revealed by SEM. These imaging techniques shed light on the link between the structure and properties of reduced graphene oxide and graphene oxide[106].

Graphene and graphene oxide quantum dots (GQDs and GOQDs, respectively) have emerged as attractive nonmaterial with unique properties and interesting applications. Understanding the dimensions, structure, surface functional groups, and optical properties of these quantum dots is crucial. This section provides important insight into the properties and potential uses of characterization methods frequently employed to examine GQDs and GOQDs. A popular method for determining the size, morphology, and composition of GQDs and GOQDs is transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 9a ). Individual quantum dots can be viewed through TEM imaging, which reveals details about their size distribution, shape, and crystallinity. Lattice fringes can be observed by high-resolution TEM, which can provide information on atomic structure and the existence of defects[107]. To examine the optical properties of GQDs, Ultraviolet-Visible (UV) and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) is often used (Fig. 9b-9c). The band gap, energy levels, and light emission properties of quantum dots are revealed by PL measurements along with their emission spectra and quantum yields. Through surface functionalization and chemical modification, PL spectroscopy enables the study of size-dependent optical behavior and modification of emission properties [107]. The composition of GQDs can be investigated using XPS measurements. A complete understanding of the bonding environment is provided by high-resolution spectra of the C 1 s peak (inset in Fig. 9d). The existence of distinct peaks at energies 284.8 eV (C = C), 286.8 eV (CO), 287.8 eV (C = O), and 289 eV (COOH) proved that the GQDs' surfaces are abundant with hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylic acid groups[108]. The good quality of the GQDs as prepared can be confirmed by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 9e). The D bands, at around 1365 cm–1, and the G band, at about 1596 cm–1, are the typical peaks of the Raman spectrum. The as-produced GQDs have a relative intensity of the "disorder" d-band and the crystalline G-band (ID/IG) of only around 0.5, which is equivalent to the intensity of high-quality few-layer graphene. This ID/IG ratio highlights the distinctiveness of the established electrochemical approach for GQD synthesis in addition to demonstrating the higher quality of the GQDs [108]. Additionally, the vibrational modes of GQDs are probed using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), which detects specific chemical functional groups. According to Fu et al. FTIR spectra reveal details of bonding properties, including the existence of oxygen-containing groups and interactions between quantum dots and other chemical species (Fig. 9f) [109]. The presence of hydroxyl and amino groups is indicated by OH and NH stretching vibrations in the range 2800–3440 cm–1, which contribute to the very hydrophilic character of GQDs. The CN bond is given a prominent band at 2340 cm–1. The C = O stretching vibration and NH vibration of the carbonyl group, which accounted for two distinct bands showed at 1760 and 1579 cm–1, respectively. A second band associated with the CN stretching vibration is also seen at 1350 cm–1.

Graphene and other variety of graphene-based materials have a wide variety of environmental applications. This application includes adsorption and degradation of pollutants, gas capture unit, desalination, and use in membrane technology. There were outstanding effects and results produced from these carbon-based materials.

Graphene structures have been widely studied in the last decade in the preparation of composites for the capture and storage of CO2, N2, and other gases[110]. With the rising concern over global warming, scientists have also found ways to separate CO2 from other gases by the use of graphene, both from greenhouse emission sources as well as directly from the atmosphere[111]. A work by Luan et al. 2022, found that crown-shaped nanopores in a graphene sheet can selectively remove CO2 while trapping other gases like N2 and O2. This selective treatment and separation would be an essential asset in the industries for flue gas treatment [111]. Other than gases the high specific surface area coverage of the graphene-based materials provides a large surface exposure for the adsorption of various pollutants. Apart from the high surface area, they also have a high constitution of surface functional groups which could be further modified for the removal of a specific pollutant in the water body [112,113,114]. The functional groups on the graphene surface play a major role (Table 1 ).

The negative functional groups tend to bind with the positive centers of the contaminant molecules while the negative centers tend to bind with the positive centers. In the last two decades, there have been numerous works based on the adsorption of various contaminants on the surface of graphene and other graphene-based composites. The list of various works in the adsorption of contaminants by graphene is described in the table below. If this material is optimized they have the potential to be used as a low-cost material for industrial-scale water purification units. Sunlight could also be utilized in the graphene-based photocatalytic material for the photodegradation of organic pollutants [132]. By looking at Fig. 10 (e) one can easily tell the effectiveness of the graphene in photodegrading different organic dyes. The work by Gunture et al. 2019 also showed that the water that is purified by photodegradation of the dyes can be further used for irrigation[133].

In the case of membrane technology, it is necessary to develop all categories of filtration units for all the different-sized particles. Besides adsorbing various contaminants graphene and its derivative have proved to have properties for the fabrication of all categories of membranes (microfiltration, ultrafiltration, nanofiltration, and reverse osmosis (RO)) [114]. By tuning the pore size and surface functionality all categories of the graphene-based membrane could be fabricated with ease. In the case of water filtration and purification, graphene-based membranes mainly RO membranes in recent years have proved effective also in the desalination units. Even though the surface of the earth comprises 75% of water, still the scarcity of fresh water is a serious challenge to humanity. The best chance for the elimination of this issue is by setting up a large-scale industrial setup of desalination units. Graphene and its derivative materials and composites have proved effective in the preparation of desalination membranes [138]. Fig. 10(a) represents a graphene-based membrane for use in the desalination process [134]. A work by Tanugi et al. in 2012 found that nanoporous graphene is effective in the obstruction of ions (mainly Na+ and Cl−) and works effectively as a desalination membrane[134]. When graphene is used as a nanoporous membrane it is able to increase the flux of salt rejection owing to the hydrophilic nature of the hydroxyl groups present in them [134]. Various theoretical studies have found that due to high chemical and mechanical stability, these graphene-based materials have more potential than traditional polymeric membranes. Another work by Surwade et al. in 2015 obtained nanoporous graphene for desalination. Here in this case the pores in the graphene were created by the bombardment of Ga+ atoms and O2 plasma. A schematic representation of the Ga+ bombardment and use of O2 plasma for the generation of nanopores in graphene is demonstrated in Fig. 10(b). The obtained membrane showed high selectivity of the rejection of Li+, Na+, K+, and Cl− ions[135]. As discussed earlier graphene can be functionalized with ease, according to the requirements. A work by Huang et al. demonstrated that by functionalization with azide, the surface of the GO can be made hydrophilic which in turn helps in designing an antifouling RO membrane. The designed RO membrane was able to reduce the fouling caused by Escherichia coli[139]. From the engineering perspective it has been found while designing a pore on the graphene-based membrane, nitrogen and fluorine terminated anionic ends at the pore aids in the rejection of the cations found in brackish water such as sodium and potassium. Whereas hydrogen terminated pores facilitates in the rejection of the common anions like fluorine, chlorine, and bromine[140]. When discussed in the terms of scalability graphene-based membranes have not been much commercialized for the desalination process. This is due to multiple combinations of positive and negatives results, and requires further studies from an industrial scale-up perspective. Here a new horizon of research has emerged which focuses on the use of photoactive materials to help in evaporation of surface water. This research trend is known as photothermal water evaporation. And due to the good electrical properties of graphene it is well suitable for this new technology.

The current technology of photothermal water evaporation is providing low cost solution for the water desalination process[141]. The materials which have the potential to efficiently utilized the solar energy and convert it to thermal energy can be used for solar water evaporation process. Photothermal materials are more effective in the conversion of surface water directly to water vapor. This method is more effective since bulk heating of water is not required[142]. Since sunlight is an abundant sustainable source, so this method also adds to sustainability. Graphene due to active electronic surface has also been used in the preparation of the photothermal system for water evaporation processes. Besides graphene's advancement in the field of RO membranes, it is also effective in desalination processes by the water evaporation method. Graphene oxide has property to transfer solar energy to heat and could be modified by the use of different polymeric materials. Bian et al. prepared a graphene oxide/polyacrylonitrile composite on cotton yarn. This tye of composite materials beisde providing the necessary support for floatability on the surface of water, has high water permeable rate. The use of polymeric materials like polyacrylonitrile is to increaese the absorptivity of solar radiation[143]. Other structure of graphene which have excellent porous nature can be an excellent choice for evaporation of water. Graphene foam is one such structure having high permeability and in a work, it was found that this type of material can have a high solar water evaporation of 278%[144]. In a work by Storer et al. in 2020 rGO was used for the preparation of a composite for solar water evaporation. Here in this work aerogels were modified by the use of rGO. To reduce the cost of preparation of the composite material rice straw fibers were used for providing the skeletal support[145].

When coming to the category of sensors graphene and its derivative materials have proven very effective in the successful preparation of different types of sensors. Due to the diverse multifunctional properties of graphene, they can be used in a variety of sensing applications, including optical sensors, electrical sensors, electrochemical sensors, chemical sensors, Etc. Depending on the requirements they can be designed. They have been used for the rapid ad precise detection of harmful chemicals, gases, and pollutants from both air and water sources. Some important properties like adsorption capacity, conductivity, tunable fluorescence, and functional groups play a major role in designing a graphene-based sensor. The high surface area of graphene-based materials provides ample surface for the adsorption of harmful chemicals and gases. When designing an electrical or electrochemical sensor it is very important to have a highly conductive support for the effective flow of the electrons. Graphene and rGO have high conductive layers for the effective transfer of electrons and could be of great use in electrochemical and electrical sensors. In the case of an electrical/electrochemical sensor, even the slightest change in the surface modification of the graphene nanostructures can affect its electronic properties. These changes are due to the change in the conductivity of the graphene nanostructures by the adsorbed analyte substances. They either act as electron donors or acceptors[146]. A similar work where rGO was used for the use of a gas sensor by the change in its resistance is shown in the Fig. 10 (c). The work by Yun et al. has shown that rGO could be used for the detection of NO2 gas at ambient temperature. The sensor prepared could be fitted on a lab coat for the detection of the harmful NO2 gas in case of a leakage. The change in resistance of the sensor was measured by the use of a digital multimeter. The sensor not only showed a high sensitivity for the NO2 sensor but also displayed high selectivity over other gases. The schematic representation of the prepared sensor is shown in the Fig. 10(c)[136].

One of highly popular field of research is the use of graphene-based materials for use of optical sensors. Mainly GQDs have been widely popular for these applications. The fluorescent nature of the GQDs due to their quantum confinement effects are the reason for the use of these materials in optical sensors. Here mainly GQDs are employed for the detection of the analytes in the water medium. Generally, it has been observed that in the presence of the analytes, there is the fluorescence quenching of the GQDs which is recorded for the sensing purpose. Efforts have been made for the preparation of naked eye fluorescent sensors for the detections of the analytes so that they could be used as a low-cost field test kit. In one the works by Anh et al. showed that nitrogen and sulfur doped GQDs were used for the fluorometric detection of the mercury (II) ions in the wastewater. The GQDs initially had blue fluorescence. With the increase in the concentration of the mercury there was gradual quenching of the fluorescence of the QDs. Efforts were also given for the preparation of the paper strips coated with the N/S doped GQDs for the use as a field test kit[53]. Similarly, in another work by Wang et al. prepared a yellow emissive nitrogen-doped GQDs which was used for the detection of Fe3+ ions. The Fig. 10(d) shown shows the synthesis methods and the graph of the pl quenching due to the Fe3+ ions. Hydrothermal methods were taken for the synthesis of the GQDs and the obtained GQDs had an average size of 3.4nm[137].

A huge section of the current research in the field of energy research in materials science is related to either based on green energy generation or the storage of energy. Of the various processes, electrochemistry and allied sciences contributes largely to both of these processes [147,148,149]. Green energy generation with the help of graphene-based materials can occur through electrochemical processes like water splitting, ammonia production, CO2 conversion to organic fuels, Etc [150]. While energy storage can be done with the advances in the research areas related to batteries (Lithium-ion battery, Sodium-ion battery, Etc) or by supercapacitors [151]. The next subsequent area of research in graphene relates to the field of electronic application. The interdisciplinary research field includes applications like solar cells, light-emitting diodes, and EMI shielding, which are gaining popularity.

At present, many minds are focused on developing certain catalysts for various electrochemical systems [152]. Catalysis is a process where a catalyst supports the chemical reaction to complete with hasten kinetics by lowering the activation energy barrier of the chemical reaction. This electrocatalytic process in energy applications can be classified into hydrogen evolution reaction (HER)[153], Oxygen Evolution Reaction (OER) [154,112], Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR)[155], Nitrogen Reduction Reaction (NRR) [148,156,157], and CO2 Reduction Reaction (CO2RR)[156]. Each of these processes is unique and has its own necessity. The HER process is used for the production of hydrogen by catalytic water splitting. The counterpart of the HER process is known as the OER process which takes place in the anode. Since the water splitting technique is governed by both HER and OER processes and the OER reaction is a slow process it is necessary to keep this reaction going[158]. In the ORR process the oxygen is reduced to water or hydrogen peroxide. This catalytic process has gained importance for its use in the preparation of fuel cell devices. The catalytic reduction of atmospheric nitrogen has several importance, firstly it can be reduced to valuable chemical products like ammonia and urea and it can also act as a good hydrogen carrier. Lastly, the most versatile and unique electrocatalytic process is the CO2 reduction reaction. As the name suggests in this process the atmospheric CO2 is reduced on the surface of the catalyst. This not only helps in the reduction greenhouse effect but also could be used for the preparation of several value-added chemical products like methanol[159], ethanol[160], methane[161], formic acid[162], etc.

Graphene can act as a very good catalyst material due to its large exposed surface area. Graphene itself has a high conductivity due to the delocalized pi electron cloud within the 2D surface area of the graphene sheet. These properties make graphene a suitable option to use separately as a catalyst for metal-free catalysis or a graphene-based catalyst composite. There are several examples where graphene or graphene-based material composites are used as catalysts for different electrocatalytic reactions. In today's world, there is a constant demand for a sustainable energy source. With catalytic water splitting, hydrogen, one of the many energy sources with the highest level of purity, may be created. At present hydrogen is generated by the steam reforming process where a lot of CO2 is also generated along with the H2. But in the case of the HER process, as the initial product of hydrogen preparation and the by-products of hydrogen combustion are the same, the production of hydrogen from water splitting has attracted attention. It was found that the purity of the generated hydrogen is around 99.9%. Long-term attempts have been made to create environmentally friendly catalysts with qualities similar to platinum. It is necessary to obtain a low-cost catalyst for industrial upscaling. The cathode which is made up of noble metal catalysts like platinum and the anode which is based on ruthenium and iridium is extremely expensive. The electrochemical reactions occurring in an electrochemical cell for HER can be represented as follows –
2H+ (aq) + 2e− → H2 (g)

In the field of electrocatalysis, the most important factor governing a process is catalyst construction. Graphene, owing to its diverse electronic and surface functional properties, has proven to aid in the catalyst construction in all the above processes [163]. Superior mechanical stability in extreme environments and low production costs have made graphene ideal for the HER process. Doping and defects on the graphene surface cause electron transfer pathway modification, ultimately affecting the catalysis process. In Fig. 11 (a) it shows that the proton penetration pathway is seriously altered by the presence of various kinds of defects and multi-layered lattices of graphene. In Fig. 11(a) the number below the graphene lattices represents the energy barrier calculated for proton penetration through the lattices [153]. A work by Najafi et al. 2019 demonstrated that few-layer irregularly shaped wrinkled graphene flakes with defective edges can act as an excellent catalytic site for water splitting[164]. HER process is popularly known to work in an acidic medium. Graphene has been well known to provide chemical stability to metal nanoparticle catalysts when they are covered in graphene. Besides providing excellent chemical stability the HER performance on the surface of graphene is nearly unaffected. This HER performance is totally dependent on the degree of graphene cover layers. A work by Hu et al. 2021 showed that an increase in the number of covers of the graphene layer alters the HER performance. Fig. 11(a) shows the corresponding DFT pattern of HER performance on different lattice forms[153].

Graphene gives us a suitable alternative to metal-based catalysis. Metal-free catalysis by graphene or non-metallic derivative-decorated graphene gives a relative safer option for catalysis, including biological catalysis. Zheng and his team prepared graphitic-carbon nitride with nitrogen-doped graphene (C3N4@NG hybrid), a graphene-supported metal-free hybrid catalyst, and reported comparable HER activity with the conventional metal catalyst [166].

Fig. 12a shows the LSV curve where the metal-free C3N4@NG hybrid shows comparable catalytic HER activity with the metal-based Pt/C catalyst. The C3N4@NG hybrid reached a certain high current density of 10 mA/cm2 at a relatively lower overpotential (approximately 240 mV) compared to the g-C3N4, N-graphene, and C3N4/NG mixture. Fig. 12b shows the Tafel plot, where the Tafel slope for the metal-free C3N4@NG hybrid (51.5 mV dec−1) is comparable with the conventional metal catalysis, which further indicates comparable HER kinetics in the presence of metal and metal-free catalysis. Fig. 12c shows LSV to represent the recyclable catalytic performance before and after 1000 catalytic cycles. The figure represents an almost similar LSV plot before and after catalytic HER in both acidic and basic mediums, which further indicates the stable nature of the catalyst. Fig. 12d shows the Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) plot, where it clearly shows the charge transfer resistance significantly decreases from the C3N4/NG mixture to the C3N4@NG hybrid. This lowering of the charge transfer resistance explains the reason behind the enhanced catalytic HER performance.

By looking at the OER reaction it might feel absurd to use water for oxygen generation, by the use of a photo/electrochemical pathway. But the OER reaction is a counter-part reaction taking place in the water splitting process. For a water-splitting process, the primary aim is the production of H2 gas. But without the continuity of the anodic OER reaction, the water-splitting reaction is incomplete. The OER process is also the slower process in the water-splitting reaction, so it has a greater influence over the faradaic efficiency of the electrolytic cell. The OER reaction can be expressed as follows –
2H2O (l) → O2 (g) + 4H+ (aq) + 4e−

Graphene is electrochemically inactive with the OER process similar to the HER reaction but doping it with suitable atoms favors the OER process. Due to the presence of the heteroatoms the electronic properties of graphene are enhanced for the OER process[167]. Lyu et al. show catalytic OER performance in the presence of graphene-based MOF composites [168]. The catalytic activity of the pristine NiFe-MOF increases significantly when it incorporates the catalyst between graphene multilayers. They explain how the enhanced conductivity due to the incorporation of the graphene system helps boost the catalytic OER process. Graphene-supported NiFe-MOF researched the current density of 10 mA/cm2 at a much lower overpotential of 106 mV compared to the pristine NiFe-MOF (399 mV).

Fig. 13
a shows the LSV curve for different catalytic systems, which clearly shows that the graphene-supported catalyst system has reached a specific current density at a much lower overpotential compared to a pristine catalyst. Fig. 13 b shows the Tafel plot, where the Tafel slope decreases from 189 mV dec−1 for pristine NiFe-MOF to 55 mV dec−1 for graphene-supported NiFe-MOF. This drop in the value of the Tafel slope further indicates enhancement of the reaction kinetics in the presence of the graphene support. Fig. 13 c shows an electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) plot where graphene-supported NiFe-MOF shows the lowest charge transfer resistance of 0.46 Ω. Fig. 13d shows the chronoamperometry (i vs. t) plot for graphene-supported NiFe-MOF for 150 h. The figure shows the catalyst efficiently retains the constant current density of 10 mA/cm2 for 150 h, which shows the stable nature of the graphene-based catalyst. Fig. 13e shows a comparison plot, which again shows that the graphene-supported NiFe-MOF reaches a higher current density of 10 mA/cm2 at a much lower overpotential compared to the other reported catalytic system.

Fuel cells, which provide a highly efficient and fuel-flexible technology that produces clean power and heat for a variety of end-uses, have the potential to significantly impact the clean energy economy. The fundamental impediment to the development of fuel cells is the slow oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). Catalysts for the oxygen reduction process (ORR) are important to key green-energy fuel cell technology. Metal-free graphene or graphene-based catalysts can also effectively catalyse the oxygen reduction process. Zhang and his team synthesised a metal-free N-doped graphene catalyst, which exhibited electrocatalytic ORR performances [169]. Catalytic activity, long-term stability, and high tolerance power make the metal-free N-doped graphene catalyst a suitable alternative to the expensive Pt-based catalyst. They also explain how the quantity of nitrogen doping over the graphene sheet directs the number of electron transfers and hence the reaction product.

Fig. 14 a shows cyclic voltammetry (CV) plots where NCM58 shows maximum reduction current density at certain overpotentials. Fig. 14b shows LSV curves for different catalytic systems. Like the CV plots, the LSV plots tell us the same story, where NCM58 shows a comparable reduction in current density at certain overpotentials with the conventional Pt/C catalyst. Fig. 14c shows Koutecky-Levich plots, which explain ORR performance in the diffusion and kinetically restricted regions. This plot, with the help of some mathematical equations, helps to determine the number of electron transfers during the ORR process. Fig. 14d shows the number of electron transfers plotted for different catalytic systems, where the number of electron transfers for the NCM58 catalyst reaches 3.7, which is very close to the number of 4 electron transfers by Pt/C during ORR. This further explains the higher catalytic nature of the metal-free, N-doped graphene catalyst.

In the middle of the rise of global warming, researchers were able to reduce atmospheric CO2 to other value-added products. So besides providing essential chemicals, CO2 reduction is also beneficial for the environment. A list of possible chemicals that can be obtained by the CO2 reduction process has been depicted in Fig. 16(a). Behind the formation of each and every chemical, there is a difference in the number of electron transfer processes. In the case of preparation of these compounds on an industrial scale where a huge amount of temperature and pressure is required, this could be totally avoided if the electroreduction process of CO2 is upscaled [170]. Similar to that of the HER process the CO2 reduction also takes place in the cathode, with the OER process taking place in the anode. In the case of CO2 reduction, it has been experimentally confirmed that because of the presence of neutral carbon atoms pristine graphene and graphene oxide are naturally inactive toward CO2 reduction. To modify the catalytic property defect assisted doping is performed. And are generally doped with heteroatoms such as boron, nitrogen, phosphorous, etc. [171]..

The graphene derivative plays a major role in another electrochemical CO2 reduction process. Tsujiguchi et al. represented enhanced electrocatalytic CO2 reduction activity by interface engineering with the help of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [172]. They incorporated rGO with the Sn catalyst and observed four times higher CO2 adsorption activity compared to the normal Sn catalyst. With the density functional theory (DFT) study, they explain how the interface of the Sn and the rGO provides a more efficient active site for CO2 adsorption and hence increases the formation of formate ions.

Fig. 15 a shows SECCM measurements that explain the correlation between the CO2 reduction activity and the local configurational nature of the Sn/rGO surface. Fig. 15b shows the LSV curve based on the different interface regions of the catalyst. This figure clearly shows a higher current density at constant overpotential at the interface of Sn/rGO, which further confirms higher CO2 reduction activity at the interface of Sn/rGO. Fig. 15c and d show a mapping of the onsite reduction current on the interface of the Sn/rGO catalyst, explaining the dependence of the reduction efficiency on the morphology.

Nitrogen is one of the prime components of the fertilizer industry and is also required for the preparation of explosives. One of the most common compounds obtained from nitrogen is ammonia and urea. They have several importance in various sects of daily life. Ammonia was first industrially commercialized during the 1st world war through the Haber-Bosch process by artificial nitrogen fixation[173]. Ammonia is widely used in the food processing industry, for the production of explosives and is also a good energy carrier in the form of hydrogen. This process of industrial ammonia production requires high temperature and pressure and additionally requires an iron catalyst. This accounts for a total of 1–3% of the total energy production of the world. While in the NRR process, atmospheric nitrogen is used and is reduced to produce ammonia. At present, there are no reports of industrial ammonia production by this method. But it could be predicted that if upscaled this method could lead to the green synthesis of ammonia. The question that remains unanswered about sustainability is the preparation of an economically sustainable electro/photocatalyst [174,175]. Aside from the challenge of catalyst design, there are also other factors that need to be checked for a higher yield of ammonia. One of the big challenges is checking the competing HER reaction. It has been observed that the HER process competes with the NRR process and prevents achieving the mass transfer limit with an increase in activity[176]. The NRR process takes place following some fundamental mechanistic pathways. They are mainly dissociative pathways, associative distal pathway, associative alternating pathway, enzymatic method, MVK methods, and surface hydrogenation method. All the mechanistic pathways are represented in Fig. 16 (b) [177]. For NRR and ORR also similar conditions were required for increasing the catalytic performance. Fig. 16(b) depicts the possible functionalization methods on graphene for preparing an NRR active catalyst[165]. In the case of the ORR processes, it was found that by doping it with heteroatoms and creating defects the ORR performance is further increased more than commercial grade platinum and carbon[178].
