Graphene-based materials also help boost the catalytic NO3RR process to produce ammonia. Adalder and his team improved the ammonia yield rate and faradic efficiency (FE) by incorporating reduced graphene oxide (rGO) into the MnPc catalyst [179]. Fig. 17 a shows the MO diagram of the MnPc catalyst, which indicates the metal centre of the catalyst has both filled HOMO and vacant LUMO. Fig. 17b shows a schematic diagram of the rGO-supported MnPc, which further indicates Π-electron transfer from rGO towards the metal centre of the MnPc catalyst. This enhanced electron density helps the conversion process of ammonia. Fig. 17c represents the ammonia yield rate vs. potential plot at different variations of the MnPc: rGO catalyst. Fig. 17d shows the FE vs. potential plot at different variations of the MnPc: rGO catalyst. 1:2 MnPc: rGO shows the maximum boost to the ammonia yield rate and FE compared to the pristine MnPc catalyst. Fig. 17e represents the 50-hour long-term stability of the MnPc-rGo catalyst. Fig. 17f shows the energy efficiency and energy consumption vs. potential plot for the catalyst. Hence, graphene-based materials help to enhance electrochemical reactions.

With the surge in the energy consumption of the world, there has been development in various fields of energy storage devices. Supercapacitors are one such device having such high power, fast charging capabilities than rechargeable batteries, longer life cycles, etc. [180,181]. Graphene was first used in supercapacitor applications in 2008[182]. There were several factors that led to the development of graphene-based supercapacitors. Firstly, the high specific surface area of graphene is an ideal choice for the preparation of electrodes. With the increase in the surface area, there is an increased possibility of ion-electrode interaction. Ease in the preparation of miniaturized micro-supercapacitors. Last but not least the low-cost fabrication technique for graphene-based supercapacitors. The electrical conductivity of graphene-based materials also is very helpful for better current collection ability and better electron transfer rate. The work by Wu et al. (2013) prepared micro-supercapacitors as demonstrated in the Fig. 18 (a). As said earlier this device on a micro-scale enables ultrahigh rate operations up to 1000 V s-1 which is three orders of magnitude faster than those of traditional supercapacitors. A recent work by Kasap et al. (2019) obtained a graphene foam-based supercapacitor that had a high-power density comparable to that of a batter and was named superbat. It is well known that with the increase in the surface area, there will be an increase in the number of active sites. Here also in the present work, the battery-like performance is one of the reasons because of the high surface area of the graphene foam[183].

Due to the presence of high conductive surface area and relatively faster charging and discharging cycles graphene has been widely used for battery designing. One of the widely researched areas is the use of graphene in the preparation of lithium-ion batteries. It has been reported that graphene helps in increasing electron conduction far greater than conventional carbon-based conductive materials. But the quantity of graphene needs to be optimized since without reasonable stacking and an excess amount can prevent the mobility of the lithium-ion[185]. Graphene also serves as a good storage material for storing lithium, and using them as an anode in lithium-ion batteries[186]. Besides the storage of lithium ions, the orientation of the graphene sheets also plays an important role in the electron transport phenomenon in an electrochemical process. This can be vertically aligned, parallelly aligned, or randomly aligned[187]. Whatever might be the orientation of the graphene sheets the objective is to maximize the electron transport process. Other nanoparticles can also be grown on the graphene surface. It has been found that different nanoparticles grown on the graphene surface can also help in enhancing the electrical conductivity of the electrode. In a work by Kamboj et al., it has been found that SnO-SnO2 nanoparticles grown on graphene can enhance the ion diffusion rate and can serve as a good anode material[188]. Other than the implementation of graphene for improving the performance of the lithium-sulfur [189] and sodium sulfur [190] batteries, graphene has also been effective in the use graphene aluminium batteries. In a work by Chen et al., they developed a graphene-based matrix for the effective channeling of ions. The prepared battery surpassed all previous types of batteries in comparison to rate capability and cycle life. The Fig. 18 (b) shows the graphene matrix as prepared, the continuous ion-conducting channel provides an excellent path for the transport of AlCl4 ions. This type of material having a quarter million cycle life and extreme flexibility can be the future of the ‘super batteries’[184]. Besides helping in the modification of different types of batteries graphene itself can also be used as a self-sufficient cathode and anode material. In a work by Kim et al., they prepared an all-graphene battery with the use of graphene cathodes and anodes. It has been said that this system is a combination of both lithium-ion batteries and supercapacitors. It is expected that these materials could be a future in carbon-based energy storage devices[191]. Yet after so much research in the field of graphene-based batteries, graphene has not been commercialized. One of the main reasons for this is the synthesis cost of graphene. However, it is expected that graphene will undoubtedly play an important role in the battery industry in the future.

Other than the energy storage applications graphene has also been extensively used in fuel cell applications. Pollution has become a global challenge for civilization as a result of the rapid expansion of industry. However, in this modern civilization, we require energy to sustain the civilization's advancement. As a result, stressing the clearer path to producing green energy is the solution to this challenge. Polymer electrolyte membrane fuel cells (PEMFC) can play an essential role in creating green energy in this manner.

Polymer electrolyte membrane fuel cells, which transform the chemical energy of fuel and oxygen into electricity, represent a promising alternative clean energy source. When a PEMFC is engineered with a polymer electrolyte membrane, such as Nafion, it helps to improve the fuel cell's performance under monitored environmental conditions, such as high proton conductivity, improved electrode kinetics, and property tailoring, as well as low carbon monoxide tolerance [192]. Here, PEMFC's engineered graphene shows enhanced performance due to the improved current density and ionic conductivity.

The most common application of graphene bridging the field of energy with the electronic applications is its use in solar cells. A solar cell, which is also known as a photovoltaic cell, is an electrical device that uses the photovoltaic effect to directly convert light energy into electricity. Graphene derivatives could potentially be used in solar cells in a number of ways to improve their performance and efficiency. One of the most commonly used materials in solar cells is ITO (Indium tin oxide), which is used as to provide conductive surface layer as well as an antireflecting coating. Since graphene is both transparent and highly conductive, it can easily replace ITO and can lead to better light adsorption and electron transport in the cell [193]. It has also been found that graphene can be used with ITO in the preparation of highly conductive Graphene/ITO transparent bi-films. The films produced by the sol-gel process demonstrated electrical conductivity with a sheet resistance of 117 Ω/sq, significantly less than that of an independent ITO film produced from sol-gel [194]. Graphene have showed its potential to be used as various components in a solar cell. Such as electrodes and charge carriers in organic solar cells (OSCs), as electron and hole transport layer as per requirement, as electrodes and solid-state electrolytes in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), Etc [195].

The engineering aspects in materials designing has showed to have a great influence on the efficiency of the solar cells. The work by Zhang et al. in 2021 showed that Ti adatoms when anchored on the rGO in Ti1O4
OH configuration can effectively tune the electronic properties of rGO [196]. The Fig. 19 a shows the schematic synthesis and structure of the Ti-anchored rGO. This property helps in preparation of cos-effective perovskite-based solar cells. The effect of tuning of the electronic property of rGO minimizes the chances of low power conversion efficiencies of the carbon-based perovskite solar cells [196]. In a recent work by Koo et al. demonstrated that polyimide integrated graphene electrodes show high flexibility and durability for use in solar cells. All thanks to the synergistic effects of graphene and polyimide, it was found that beside having high optical transmittance over 92% the designed materials exhibited extraordinary mechanical robustness up to 10,000 bending cycles similar to that of the solar cells based on ITO as a reference [197]. The schematic representation of the two step synthesis process by CVD and spin coating for the graphene/polyimide electrodes are shown in Fig. 19b The materials also had an ultraclean surface which showed an optical transmittance of greater than 92%. Similar to the other application the main challenges in implementation of graphene in solar cell application is its ease of effective synthesis. It is expected that due to its flexible nature, low resistivity, high optical transparency, and good conductive support it would surely be used in the solar cells in future.

Since graphene-based composites are lightweight, versatile, and have excellent mechanical and electrical properties, they are becoming increasingly attractive for use in electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding applications [198,199]. Some important discoveries and applications of graphene-based composites in EMI shielding are as follows:

Thin graphene/PMMA nanolaminates: Nanolaminates composed of thin layers of graphene and poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) exhibit notable electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding capabilities. With an EMI shielding effectiveness of 60 dB and a minimal thickness of 33 μm, these materials demonstrate impressive performance. Additionally, their absolute EMI shielding effectiveness, approaching 3.105 dB cm2 g − 1, is one of the highest recorded for non-metallic materials up to the present date [198].

Graphene-based thermoplastic composites: Experiments have been conducted on thermoplastic composites containing graphene, focusing on their electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding and thermal conductivity properties. The analysis contained significant graphene content, reaching concentrations of up to 50 wt percent (wt%) [200].
Graphene and graphene oxide: Research has explored the viability of using graphene, graphene oxide, and doped graphene for applications related to electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding [201].

Graphene and silver nanowires: Graphene combined with silver nanowires has been investigated for its cost-effectiveness and exceptional electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding efficiency in composite materials [201].

3D graphene network with PDMS: A three-dimensional graphene network, when paired with PDMS, attained significant electrical conductivity (6100 S/m) with a modest graphene content of 1.2 wt%. This composite displayed notable electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding effectiveness, reaching approximately 40 and 90 dB within the X-band range, maintaining a thickness of 0.25 mm [202].

Graphene sponge with polyurethane: In research conducted by Hu et al. [203], a self-assembled composite of graphene and polyurethane sponge was produced using a two-step hydrothermal method, showcasing the potential for electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding. A composite material consisting of a graphene sponge and polyurethane attained a shielding effectiveness of 35 dB with a sample thickness measuring 9 mm and a graphene loading of 18.7 wt%. This material exhibited a noteworthy specific EMI shielding effectiveness ranging from 969 to 1578 dB cm² g⁻¹, comparable to or surpassing conventional graphene/polymer sponges. The outstanding EMI shielding performance was attributed to graphene's superconductivity and the highly porous structure of the graphene/polyurethane sponge. The polyurethane sponge played a crucial role as a robust framework, facilitating the formation of graphene's three-dimensional structure.

These investigations showcase the promise of using graphene-based composites in electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding applications, presenting a combination of excellent shielding effectiveness and the benefits of being lightweight and flexible. Nevertheless, there are ongoing challenges in finding a harmonious equilibrium among factors such as electromagnetic shielding efficiency, thickness of shielding materials, durability, mechanical strength, minimized volume, weight, and elasticity.

There are several metal composites that are generally used for commercial LEDs and displays. Material with high conductivity and flexibility is required for the LED and display applications. Rare-earth material composites are generally used for this kind of LED and display application [204,205]. However, there have long been concerns about its utilisation due to the restricted availability of this rare earth material in the earth's crust. Here, graphene or graphene-based composites give us a favourable alternative to these rare earth composites for use as LEDs and display applications due to the excellent flexibility and electrical conductivity of the graphene or graphene-based composites [206,207]. Weng et al. show graphene, a low-cost fabrication material, to replace the popular rare earth-based indium tin oxide (ITO) for the LED application [208].

Fig. 20a shows the voltage vs. current density characteristic curve for HNO3-doped and undoped single-layer graphene (SLG) OLEDs compared with ITO OLEDs. Fig. 20b represents the voltage vs. irradiance plot, which clearly indicates NHO3-doped single-layer graphene (SLG) shows comparable performance with the commercial ITO OLED material. Fig. 20c represents the current density vs. internal quantum efficiency plot, which clearly indicates that a HNO3-doped SLG shows comparable and, to some extent, high quantum efficiency after a certain high current density value. Fig. 20d shows a pictorial representation of the OLEDs working at 5 V.

Various disciplines in the field of science apart from Biology have often shaken hands together when it has come to the remediation of the problems in healthcare and disease for the greater good of humanity. The application of graphene and its derivatives as the materials engineered for applications in the field of biomedical research is a pertinent example of the above [209,210]. This is attributed to the fact that Graphene can be deemed to be one of the strongest materials that have ever been presented in front of mankind. This qualitative statement is backed up quantitatively with regards to the mechanical, electrical, optoelectronic, optical and physicochemical properties of this wonder material. The subsequent discussion is dedicated to the contributions of Graphene and its derivative materials in the field of biomedical science.

2D nanomaterials like Graphene has often taken the centre stage in Drug Delivery Systems (DDSs). Modifications, however have been always necessary to serve our purpose. This is because Graphene in itself has its intrinsic disadvantages such that its biocompatibility is compromised, thanks to the low dispersibility, low functionality and the debated toxicity of Graphene-based nanomaterials[211]. Yang et al. in 2008 showed that anticancer drugs like Doxorubicin Hydrochloride (DXR) can be loaded on GO and pH dependent loading and release can be brought about[212]. The GO used in this study was a product of modified Hummers method and was obtained as a brown powder. GO is generally preferred over Graphene itself because of the fact that it has way more functionality as compared to Graphene and that aids in enhancing its solubility in water. Not only that, GO offers a very balanced proportion between its chemical properties that are exploitable with regards to drug loading and its biocompatibility. The planar structure comprising of six-membered carbon rings aid in the π-π interactions of Graphene with the aromatic structures of the various drugs aimed at loading on it. It was loaded with DXR via sonication, maintaining the concentration and the pH. Overnight stirring and ultracentrifugation at 14,000 rpm emanated the DXR-loaded-GO, keeping in mind that the pH was kept under 6 due to the fact that highest loading capacity was observed at pH 6. There was a linear increment of the loading of DXR with the increase in the initial DXR concentration as high as upto 2.35 mg/mg at the initial concentration of 0.47 mg/mL. The release of the drugs were monitored by dialysis in aqueous solution followed by quantification using UV Spectroscopy. It was concluded that drug release became optimal under acidic conditions since the hydrogen bonding interactions between DXR and GO was maximal around the neutral pH. At pH 2, 71% of the bound DXR was released from the nanohybrid. The particulars with regards to the data has been summarised in Fig. 21 .

The same group in 2009 demonstrated that Graphene Oxide can be coupled with Fe3O4 nanoparticles to form a hybrid such that it can be used to achieve controlled and targeted drug delivery[213]. This theorized the idea that Graphene is a potential candidate for a variety of modifications at the nano-scale to achieve targeted and controlled drug delivery. The carboxylate content of the synthesized GO was subsequently determined by acid-base titration. These groups after sonication with alkali were transformed to their anions, which were used for the subsequent for the capturing of Fe2+/Fe3+ ions when Ferrous and Ferric salts were added. The ions coordinated with the Graphene sheet got subsequently converted to NPs when subjected to aqueous NaOH solution. The drug, here also DXR, was loaded onto the GO with the loading capacity maximising at 1.08 mg/mg. Congregation of the DXR@GO-Fe3O4 occurred at acidic pH and redispersion under basic conditions. This aided in the pH-controlled release of the DXR along with its customised directionality due to the superparamagnetic nature of the nanohybrids, as portrayed in Fig. 21. These types of work acquainted the scientific community to the potential of Graphene and its derivatives as effective small-molecule drug carriers.

The potentiality of Graphene in general and GO in particular is attributed to the surface properties and the surface chemistry of the nanomaterial. For molecules like DXR, the planarity, the subsequent π-π stacking interactions and hydrophobic interactions play a vital role in giving Graphene-Family Nanomaterials (GFNs) their name as an effective drug carrier. The beauty of its applicability is in the feature that GFNs can interact with a variety of biomolecules[214]. This is applicable for rGO too, which was exemplified by the work of Kim et al. in which Doxorubicin (DOX) was loaded on to PEG-BPEI-rGO, with an efficacy reported to be higher than that for PEG-BPEI-GO[215]. The mechanism of the drug being taken up by the cells is via the formation of an endosome. This endosome-mediated delivery of drug has to reach completion by the release of the drug into the cell via disruption of the endosomal membrane. Since degradation of the drug is a possibility, successful release was ensured by photothermally triggered liberation of DOX into the cells. The radiation used was in the NIR range of wavelength, and was coupled with GSH-accelerated release of DOX to demonstrate that the nanohybrid can be used to attain target-specific as well as photothermally controllable drug delivery, escaping the endosome photothermally and also via the proton-sponge effect of BPEI [215,216]. Thereon, it was shown that the drugs killed more cancer cells with NIR than without it (Fig. 22 (a,b,c)).

Apart from Doxorubicin, Quercetin (QC) has also been delivered effectively by Najafabadi et al., with the help of their designed nanocarriers composed of gelatin (G)-polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) coated graphene oxide (GO)[218]. The nanocomposite was also coated with a double emulsion for attaining a higher control over drug release. The release was again pH dependent, being higher in the acidic medium as compared to that in the neutral medium, and the rate of cancer cell death was estimated at 53.14% with a 45% drug loading. Water soluble GQDs have been synthesized for inhibition of autoimmune diseases like HIV, by investigating the anti-HIV activity of graphene as well as its conjugated form with two non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs), namely CHI499 and CDF119[219]. This research group showed that Graphene can be employed as an effective agent for HIV treatment, with GQD-CHI499 turning out to be an effective RTI-conjugated nanomaterial showing an IC50 of 0.09 µg/mL and an EC50 value in cells of 0.066 µg/mL. In recent years grapheneoxide quantum dots have also proven effective in the drug delivery applications [220].

In addition to the fact that drugs have been shown to be effectively targeted and released into the tumour cells, often dual drug combinations like Doxorubicin and Irinotecan have been loaded onto GO for their stimuli-responsive release into the cancer cells[221]. henceforth, this combination was reported to be showing higher levels of cytotoxicity in comparison to loading with a single drug. But scientific research has evidenced that Graphene and its derivatives, without the contribution of any drug, can also act as a chemotherapeutic agent for the treatment of cancer. One of the ways that cancer cells thrive is by avoiding apoptosis via the manipulation of the defective machinery of the affected cells and often, of the surrounding cells. But targeted DNA damage can be a very effective way to induce apoptosis in the cells contributing to the neoplastic growth. Targeted action Henceforth, is necessary and is provided by one of the members of GFNs, the GQDs. Qi et al. [222] have shed light upon this property of GQDs by performing its amine-functionalisation and performing subsequent modifications such that the GQDs, to be introduced to the neoplastic cells, undergo proper training so as to identify which cells it has to infiltrate and kill.

Firstly, the amine functionalised QDs were prepared hydrothermally, followed by modification with nucleus-targeting TAT peptides. Secondly, the synthesized TAT-NGs were grafted with Folic Acid (FA) modified PEG (FAPEG), so as to direct them to the cervical cancer HeLa cells. The overexpressed Folic Acid receptors were exploited to be the markers for the FAPEG-TNGs and without the FAPEG modification, the QDs were non-selectively cytotoxic. The resultant therapeutic effect of the FAPEG-TNGs was good, as they found both in vivo and in vitro. Schematic outline of the synthesis of FAPEG-TNGs and their mechanism of action is depicted in Fig. 23 (a,b). The cause of cytotoxicity was the expression of pro-apoptotic proteins like p53, pi-p53, Bax and Caspase-3, and repression of the anti-apoptotic proteins like Bcl-2. Not only chemotherapy, but also scientific research on combinational cancer therapy has benefitted from 2D nanomaterials like Graphene. One of the examples include a combination of radiotherapy and photothermally induced anti-cancer activity using Fe3O4@Au/reduced GO[223], with good resultant biocompatibility and cytotoxicity. Also, chemo-phototherapy and chemo-photodynamic therapies have been combined to excellent effect in cancerous cells to induce apoptosis with remarkable selectivity. Fig. 23 (c,d) shows how Graphdiyne oxide (GDYO) exhibits cytotoxicity against leukemia cells, which was demonstrated by Wang et al. [224], highlighting the fact that GDYO nanosheets can also deliver anti-cancer properties, and have performed better in terms of cytotoxicity as compared to GO. Through this work, they also demonstrated that GDYO also possess in vivo therapeutic potential with regards to killing the leukemia cells, when administered in NSG mice. GDYO interacted with Cell-Adhesion Molecules (CAMs), triggered the disassembly of the cytoskeletal network of the cancer cells and induced apoptosis in them.

Diseases like COVID-19 have pointed out the loopholes in biomedical technology in combating pathogens when they present themselves in different attires, amongst many others. So, application of such a versatile nanomaterial like GFNs to combat the pandemic has not escaped the scientific minds even amid the sprint for synthesizing vaccines all over the world. Molecular dynamics simulations have shown that pristine multi-layered Graphene can interact with the spike proteins of the SARS-CoV-2 virus [226]. Composites with other nanoparticles (not belonging to GFNs) can also yield anti-viral properties or can facilitate its functioning. Researchers have shown that magnetic nanoparticles when loaded onto rGO, have better anti-viral properties as compared to when they are administered without rGO. There have been reports where functionalised GQDs were targeted at Feline Coronavirus (FCoV NTU156) and Enterovirus (EV71)[225].

The functionalised GQDs showed exceptional inhibition properties, such that the functionalised GQDs could either be sprayed on contaminated surfaces exerting its anti-viral properties as shown in Fig. 23(e), in which case they exhibit their anti-viral effect by disrupting the lipid membrane, or can used to fabricate anti-viral surfaces coated with the functionalised GQDs which initiate their action when they come in contact with the FCoV NTU156 or the EV71 and therefore prevent them from contaminating the surfaces, as shown in Fig. 23 (f, g). So, the functionalised GQDs can also be used as a disinfectant spray just like the sanitizer sprays, the use of which has gained popularity thanks to COVID-19. The work has unveiled the vast potential of the GFNs in times of such distress as presented by the pandemic.

Exploring the vast realm of applications of a material like Graphene and the plethora of derivatives that can be synthesized, is a never-ending task at hand, but one of them that needs a mention in the biomedical field is biosensing. The pandemic witnessed a rapid increment in the use and development of Point-Of-Care testing (POCT). Fig. 24 (a) below shows such a POCT scheme, where a Graphene Field Effect Transistor (GFET) biosensor has been developed by Prattis et al. [227], which is sensitive to the spike protein of the SARS-CoV-2. The GFET was designed such that the antibodies for the spike proteins were immobilised onto the surface. The immobilisation was done with the help of 1-pyrenebutyric acid N-hydroxysuccinimide ester (PBASE), one of the popular reagents used for immobilizing proteins. Soaking in PBASE was followed by incubation of the device for 4 h, after its exposure to SARS-CoV-2 spike protein antibodies. The limit of detection (LOD) obtained was as low as 1 fg/mL, signifying higher sensitivity than ELISA test. Simply, the antibody interacts with the surface antigen and a change in the signal is recorded. The COVID-19 FET, as they called it, also showed insignificant signal alteration in case of MERS-CoV spike proteins, stating that the fabricated device has selectivity in addition to sensitivity. One of the important vitals that are monitored in patients is the blood pressure. Continuous monitoring of the same can also be done via GFNs, as demonstrated by Kireev et al. (Fig. 24(b),(c),(d)) in 2022, where they designed graphene bioimpedance tattoos for continuously monitoring the blood pressure non-invasively[228]. Just as the resistance is decreased when the cross-sectional diameter of a conducting medium increases, bioimpedance decreases with the increase in the BP owing to the increase in the arterial volume. This ΔZArtery is measured in a cyclical manner, analogous to the cyclic nature of ECG, and the BP is evaluated. They have achieved accuracies which correspond to the standards set by the IEEE, which has made the device equivalent to a Grade A wearable measuring device (0.06±2.5 mmHg (DBP) and 0.2 ± 3.6 mmHg (SBP) for a cycling-trained machine learning regression model, and 0.2 ± 4.5 mmHg (DBP) and 0.2 ± 5.8 mmHg (SBP) for HGCP training). The device portrays itself as the blueprint for fully integrated wearable system, such as a smart watch.

ATP and GTP molecules are integral to the functioning of biological systems and needless to say, that their monitoring needs attention in assessing the current status of the maintenance of homeostasis, owing to their roles in metabolism, cell signaling, neurotransmission and muscle contraction, to name some of them. Imaging of ATP in cells and tissues by using molecular probes has introduced us to a plethora of materials like two-photon probes and rhodamine-lactam probes[229]. Graphene has not failed the scientific community in this case also, since Wang et al. have shown that GO nanosheets (GO-nS) can be used in multiple florescence monitoring of ATP and GTP [230]. When coupled with DNA/RNA aptamers, these can produce fluorescence upon interaction with the nucleoside triphosphates inside the cell, which can then be visualised. The biocompatibility and toxicity studies were also conducted and the salient features of this method includes high sensitivity, specificity for structurally similar molecules and no requirement of transfection.

Out of the five primary senses that a human possesses, sight is probably the most important sense that is required when we aim to understand a dynamic biological process involving the movement of biomolecules. A sophisticated terminology for visualising such movements is known as bioimaging. Not only movement, but only for the sake of viewing certain components of the cell or observing the cellular organisation, if required, also falls under bioimaging. Optical, electronic, and magnetic properties of Graphene are some of the most important properties that are exploited when GFNs are used for bioimaging applications. We have seen that decorated GFNs can be very effective small-molecule drug carriers. But we can also use them to visualise the cellular uptake of the therapeutic agent by the target cells, the distribution of it, and in the field of oncology, we can use the GFNs in a manner so as to detect tumours in their early stages, even to observe metastasis and resurgence of the cancer for that matter. At the start of the previous decade, GQDs have been shown to have excellent applicability with regards to bioimaging, with a facile synthesis procedure and a high quantum yield of 11.4%[231]. The key factors of this work apart from the synthesis methodology are the high stability, bright fluorescence, and the low cytotoxicity of the GQDs. How the group has imaged translocation of the GQDs through the cell membranes, exploiting the excitation dependent PL behaviour of the GQDs has been depicted in the Fig. 25 (b,c,d) below. Scientists have managed to prepare nanocomposites with tuneable emissions for in-vivo bioimaging. AgInZnS-Graphene Oxide (AIZS-GO) nanocomposite has been made, such that hydrophobic AgInZnS QDs (AIZS QDs) have been associated with hydrophilic GO to prevent the QDs from aggregating or precipitating out in biological systems[232]. The AIZS QDs were assembled upon the oleylamine modified GO by a micro-emulsion technique. The PL behaviour ranged from green to red emission. SK-BR-3 breast cancer cells tumour-bearing nude mice were taken for the in vivo imaging of the cancer cells after checking the biocompatibility and photostability of the as synthesized AIZS-GO in vitro. Laser irradiation for varied amounts of time resulted in red fluorescence from the nanocomposite, signifying the location of the tumour. Organs were also harvested from the tumour containing mouse and the fluorescence imaging was performed, hinting at the successful and proper distribution of the nanocomposite into the various organs of the body (Fig. 25 (e, f, g)). The bioimaging studies were conducted both in vitro and in vivo, and the results obtained showed a fair extent of biocompatibility, photostability and low toxicity. The nanocomposite provides a means of biomedical imaging using non-invasive techniques.

October 8th, 1958 was the day when World's first successful pacemaker implantation was carried out at the Karolinska Hospital, Stockholm, Sweden, thanks to the contributions of the surgeon Ake Senning and the physician inventor Rune Elmqvist[233]. Since then, technology in healthcare has seen massive progress, where we have seen the applications of novel materials and devices with innumerable variations in their design and mechanism of action. Epidermal Electronic Systems (EESs) have been developed, which have the unique property of matching the thickness and the mechanical properties of the epidermis[234]. Graphene Electronic Tattoos (GETs) have been fabricated so as to monitor important physiological biomarkers, such as the cardiac rhythm in case of an ECG. Only two GETs are required to be placed either on the chest or on two arms of the patient to continuously monitor the heart rhythm. As mentioned in one of the previous sections, Graphene tattoos have also been developed for monitoring the blood pressure. The potential of Graphene is not limited to applications like this, as shown by Lin et al., since they have applied this concept of Graphene tattoos to create a bioelectronic interface such that not only does it monitor heart rhythms, but can also stabilize them when discrepancies arise, as in patients with cardiac arrhythmia[235]. The Graphene was grown as a one-atom thick layer via Chemical Vapour Deposition technique. The pacing function of the tattoos were demonstrated in vivo by conducting experiments on rat as the model organism of choice. The experiments showed that after inducing a Atrio-Ventricular block (AV block) by intraperitoneal injection of measured amounts of Caffeine and Dobutamine, when no pacing treatment was applied, the rat heart went into 4:1 AV block. The heart rhythm was redeemed after ventricular pacing by the GET-electrode, and was evidenced by the continuous electrocardiographic monitoring of the heart, which exhibited normalcy once the heart was subjected to the pacing activity of the GETs. This work has been summarized pictorially in Fig. 26 (a) with regards to how the pacemaker evolved over time, and utilizes Graphene in soft bioelectronics, albeit proper scrutiny of the device as being fit for implantation in higher mammals like humans, is necessary and in accordance with the authors, it may induce some changes in the design and the equipment composition of the device itself.

In serious conditions such as end-stage renal disease (ESRD), dialysis acts essentially like a life support, and Graphene has shown promise in this domain also. Researchers have shown via molecular simulation studies that Graphene nanosheets when doped with appropriate percentage of nitrogen can be used as an effective adsorbent of urea, hence serving as a promising candidate in urea removal and in enhanced dialysis (schematically represented in Fig. 26(b)). When investigated via simulations, graphene nanosheets containing 10% nitrogen has shown to adsorb urea optimally with regards to the total energy and adsorption energy[236,237]. The study was aimed at laying out the optimal experimental conditions for the graphene nanosheets to adsorb urea effectively. Eye drops in cases of eye diseases like keratoconjunctivitis sicca is a commonplace go to medicinal solution. But, it does come with many a disadvantage such as blurry vision, repeated and closely monitored dosing and limited bioavailability. It is therefore a quick and effective remedy to administer the eye drops via contact lenses. To be technically apt, scientists have devised a method by which they can design drug-eluting contact lenses with the additional advantage of incorporating GO into it thereby enhancing the drug uptake of the lens as a whole[237]. The idea was to see whether GO had any intrinsic advantage in its delivery of the drug continually, starting from the uptake of the drug. To investigate this, the group fabricated the hydrogel contact lenses made of a copolymer of N,N-Dimethylacrylamide and hydroxyethyl methacrylate with ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) crosslinker and n-vinyl pyrrolidone as the plasticizer, in various configurations. The control was taken as the normal contact lens soaked in cyclosporine. For studying the degree of enhancement of drug uptake, opto-physical properties and the release kinetics, GO loaded contact lenses were fabricated and subsequently soaked in the drug.

Due to its excellent flexibility, electrical, optical, and thermal properties, it is suitable for various applications. The high aspect ratio and large specific surface area make it more suitable for catalytic application solely or as a composite with other catalytic substances. Graphene or graphene-based material gives us an alternative to metal-free catalysis.

For gas sensing, graphene sheet imperfections are crucial. [238] Using a theoretical first-principles simulation, the gas sensing characteristics of pure, defective, and doped graphene have been examined [239]. The findings show that faulty graphene reacts poorly with CO, NO, or NO2, but strongly with NH3. While boron-doped (B-doped) graphene demonstrates improved interactions with NO, NO2, and NH3, nitrogen-doped (N-doped) graphene showed high binding with NO2. There are several examples where a graphene sheet acts as a chemical sensor. Graphene, a graphene-based material, can be used in various kinds of biosensors, such as chemo-resistive sensors, humidity sensors, electrochemical biosensors, electric biosensors, optical biosensors, etc. Salehi-Khojin prepared randomly stacked graphene flecks and showed the application as a chemical sensor. [240]

Fig. 27 a shows the variation of device conductance with the fractional volume of the element. This figure indicates that the system used in this study performed consistently and had a small standard deviation. Fig. 27b shows resistance or conductance dependence on temperature. This figure displays the graphene sensors' resistance, normalised to room temperature for the 100–300 K temperature range. Fig. 27c provides more insight into the semiconducting-like SEG films' thermally-assisted transport process by closely examining the resistance in the 100–400 K temperature range. A linear fit to ln(R) vs. T- 1/3 is found for the 5 ml sensor in the insert of Fig. 27c. Fig. 27d shows a high-resolution TEM image of the randomly distributed graphene flakes. Fig. 27e shows the diffraction pattern in the inner region of the graphene flakes from the TEM image, which represents the hexagonal structure of crystalline graphene. Fig. 27f shows the diffraction pattern in the outer region (edge) of the graphene flakes from the TEM image, which represents distinctive rings as a result of long-range order being lost.

Graphene-based materials can work as mechanically responsive materials. The hexagonal structure of graphene partially deforms under tensile strain, changing the electronic band and, consequently, the resistance of the graphene sheet [241,242,243,244]. Thus, intrinsic graphene has great potential as a material for piezoresistive strain sensors. Fu et al. Explain how the graphene-based elastic material displayed a decrease in resistance when strain was applied, which was basically for the relaxation of pre-existing wrinkles in the graphene film. However, the distortion of the hexagonal crystal structure of graphene resulted in an increase in resistance [245].

The strain dependence of the electrical characteristics of the graphene sheet on the PDMS substrate is depicted in Fig. 28 . Fig. 28 a represents a schematic diagram of the strain-dependent electrical graphene device. Fig. 28b shows the current-voltage I-V curve of the graphene-based device under different tensile strains. Fig. 28c represents the variation of the resistance with the percentage of the strain. Fig. 28d represents the relative variation of the resistance and the experimental fitting curve with a percentage of the strain.

Graphene has a negative coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) [246]. This property is unique in comparison with conventional positive CTE materials. And GO has more negative CTE than pristine graphene. This makes GO a valuable and unique material to use in thermosensitive devices. Ren and his team describe a novel approach to creating a thermo-adaptive material with graphene sheets coated with polymers [247]. Figs. 29 a and 29b define the swelling ratio (SR) as the ratio of tube diameter (D) to edge length (L) of the platelet in order to assess the rolling and unrolling process. In Fig. 29 c, which was derived from Fig 29, a and b, the SR of the samples was plotted versus temperature for a heating and cooling procedure.

Graphene, due to its flexible and adoptive nature, can easily be compatible with other materials to use in the devise field device application. Incorporating ferromagnetic material makes it applicable for the magneto-response material. Yang et al. explain magnetic field-controlled drug delivery by using a Fe3O4-graphene composite. This kind of material is also used in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) devices [248]. Apart from the above-described applications, graphene-based materials can be used for optical sensor devices, catalytic applications, sheet applications for microdevices, etc. This is basically possible due to the presence of such unique characteristics in graphene. The flexibility and electrically conductive nature make it a suitable option for electronic devices. The flexibility and thermal conductivity make it suitable for the thermo-sensing device application. Flexibility and excellent compatibility make it easy for researchers to make graphene-based composites for several device applications, such as photo-sensing and magneto-sensing devices. It's large surface area and aspect ratio, good conductivity, and flexible nature make it a good catalyst material for electrocatalysis. We can obtain different properties by engineering the structure of the graphene sheet or modifying graphene with additional moieties (GO, rGO, and functionalized graphene), which makes it suitable for application in different dimensions of the field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Industrial aspects of graphene, scalability, and future prospects from the industrial

ce:section.ce:para:
A variety of industrial applications find graphene attractive due to its unique combination of remarkable properties.
(a)
The high electrical conductivity and electron mobility in graphene holds promise for ultrafast transistors, flexible displays, and high-performance touch screens in the electronics industry[249]. Due to its excellent electrical properties, graphene is a strong contender for advances in electronics and optoelectronics. High-performance transistors, integrated circuits, and flexible electronic devices with high electron mobility, improved charge carrier transport, and configurable bandgaps now pave the way. Next-generation electronics are now possible because of graphene field-effect transistors (GFETs), which have shown extremely high carrier mobility, fast switching times, and low power consumption[217].
(b)
Supercapacitors and lithium-ion batteries made of graphene-based materials promise significant advances in energy storage by increasing energy density and charging speed[250].
(c)
In addition, graphene's large surface area and biocompatibility have opened the door to novel uses in drug delivery systems and bio-sensing devices in the healthcare sector[251].
(d)
Graphene is an excellent contender for reinforcing composites in the aerospace and automotive industries due to its high mechanical strength and lightweight design[252,253]).
(e)
Additionally, research into graphene-based photodetectors, solar cells, and light-emitting devices has been stimulated by graphene's transparency and broad absorption spectrum. Graphene is an important enabler for developments in the semiconductor and photonics sectors due to its unique blend of electrical and optical capabilities.

The scalable production of graphene is crucial for its effective incorporation into industrial applications. Several synthesis methods have been developed over the years to produce large amounts of graphene, including chemical vapour deposition (CVD), exfoliation methods, and solution-based techniques. Since it makes it possible to synthesize high-quality graphene sheets on substantial substrates, CVD stands out as the most promising of these methods for industrial scaling. However, affordability, reproducibility, and homogeneity of the generated graphene still exist. Recent developments in process optimization and continuous roll-to-roll fabrication have facilitated the realization of large-scale graphene production for industrial applications. Numerous studies have focused on the scalability of graphene, and scientists have explored several techniques to increase production while maintaining the quality of the graphene material. As an example, Strudwick et al. [254]. & Li et al. [29]. suggested a continuous manufacturing method using a dual-feed CVD system, enabling scalable synthesis of high-quality graphene sheets with controlled thickness and electrical properties. Furthermore, Bae et al. [249]. developed a roll-to-roll manufacturing technique that is cost-effective and scalable for fabricating graphene-based conductive films, opening up a promising new market for flexible electronics applications.

Future key aspects: At current market graphene has been known to be used in several industry grade processes. The size of the global graphene market was estimated at USD 175.9 million in 2022, and it is anticipated to rise at a CAGR of 46.6% from 2023 to 2030. The market is anticipated to be driven by two factors: the rising penetration of composite applications and the expanding electronics sector in emerging economies [255]. At present the applications of graphene like that in battery and supercapacitor in industrial scale is limited due to various factors like higher energy barrier and safety requirements, but it is expected to grow in the near future.

(a) It has been found that graphene has been used in the development of cooling system in mobile phones [256]. Recently a graphene-based rubber has been developed by the SpaceBlue, and it is said to be highly environmentally friendly, and has been made from recycled tyre and natural rubber. Besides providing aid to the waste management, the mat produced has 5 times more strength compared to other rubber products [257]. The industry applications are not limited to the environmental, energy, and biomedical products. One of the common industrial preferences of graphene is its mechanical properties which have facilitated its use in the development of various which require high strength. One such example is use of graphene in tennis racquet.

(b) Presence of graphene gave the racquets better mass distribution ultimately leading to better manoeuvrability and more power. This was very successful and the racquets produced were used in prestigious competitions like Grand Slam, ATP masters 1000 tournament, WTA premier mandatory etc. [258].

(c) Graphene has also been found to be used in automobile industries in the production of tires and lubricants. Industrial commercialization is big step. It took around 70 years for successful commercialization of the lithium-ion battery. It is expected that the future of graphene in industry is bright and hold numerous possibilities of industrial products [259].

(d) In energy-storage devices such as batteries and supercapacitors, graphene-based materials exhibit outstanding performance. Expanded energy density, faster charging, and extended cycle life are among the advantages of developing graphene-based electrodes and energy storage devices [260].

(e) Due to its unique physicochemical properties and biocompatibility, graphene is an attractive option for biomedical applications, such as drug administration, biosensing, and imaging. Subsequent investigations may provide ground-breaking medical and diagnostic solutions [261].

(f) Environmental preservation and water purification can benefit from the superior absorption properties of graphene-based products. Creating effective materials based on graphene that can solve global environmental problems may happen in the future [262].

Existing challenges: Despite graphene's enormous promise, a number of issues need to be resolved before it can be widely used in industry or daily use.
(a)
Large-scale production's scalability and cost-effectiveness are still ongoing research topics. The manufacturing of defect-free graphene and exact control over the electrical band structure both continue to be difficult problems. To guarantee the safe and dependable inclusion of graphene in commercial goods, standardization, and regulatory concerns are also essential.
(b)
Additionally, a detailed assessment of the effects of the manufacturing and disposal of graphene on the environment is necessary in order to create sustainable production methods[263,264]. Even with these obstacles, recent advances in the production of graphene and our understanding of its properties open the door to revolutionary possibilities in various sectors. To fully realize the promise of graphene and advance its incorporation into various industrial applications, researchers, industry stakeholders, and policymakers need to work together.
(c)
The future prospects of this amazing material appear increasingly promising across numerous industrial areas as industrial research and development activities continue to improve graphene production and tailor its properties.
(d)
The cost-effectiveness and economic feasibility of graphene-based products are increasing with the improvement of scalable manufacturing techniques, making it increasingly feasible for large-scale applications. To ensure compliance with safety standards and enable the use of graphene in various industrial processes, joint efforts between academia, industry, and regulatory authorities will be crucial in determining the future of graphene's industrial applications.

ce:list-item.ce:label: (a)
ce:list-item.ce:para: Developing scalable and environmentally friendly techniques for graphene, such as liquid-phase exfoliation and chemical vapour deposition, should be a major focus of research efforts.

ce:list-item.ce:label: (b)
ce:list-item.ce:para: To ensure repeatability and reliability, collaborative efforts within the scientific community should define standard operating procedures for the synthesis, characterization, and reporting of materials based on graphene.

ce:list-item.ce:label: (c)
ce:list-item.ce:para: Solving difficult problems requires a diverse strategy. Researchers from different disciplines, such as chemistry, physics, biology, and material science, can work together to accelerate the study of graphene and its potential uses.

ce:list-item.ce:label: (d)
ce:list-item.ce:para: To ensure the safe development and application of graphene-based products, proactive regulatory and risk assessment frameworks should be established. This requires monitoring of potential impacts on the environment and human health.

ce:section.ce:para: In the preceding two decades graphene and other graphene-based materials have proved themselves worthy on so many grounds. Graphene-based materials have gained more importance compared to other widely researched carbon-based materials like fullerene, carbon nanotubes, carbon black, carbon nitride etc. Now one might ask this question ‘Why graphene?’ instead of so many other fascinating carbon-based materials could be answered from the title itself. It has been boldly expressed to be ‘Jack of all trades’, which when judged from the diverse list of application mentioned in this review and presence of various physiochemical properties is quite appropriate. But under the current circumstances’ graphene-based materials are far from getting the throne of ‘Jack of all trades’ when judged from the commercial point of view. The biggest barrier in terms of scalability of a product comes from the cost survey. It is quite true that graphene is better and has gained more popularity that the other advance classes of carbon-based materials in laboratory research. But still more research works on the upscaling possibility is required for use as an industry grade material. Currently on the basis of the various lab scale graphene engineering process going across the globe it is expected that graphene-based products could be implemented at commercial scale in the near future. The present challenges that remain is to design more cost viable fabrication techniques for all kind of graphitic systems like graphene, GO, rGO, GQD, and GOQD. Especially it would be more challenging to effectively prepare near perfect pristine graphene monolayer. Coming to the engineering aspect more studies are required to optimize a system with a balance in the defect contents and dopants so as to get the desired results. It is important to design more cost-effective composites and membranes by the use of graphene for better storage and filtration material compared for other classes of materials like MOFs. In the biomedical field it is essential to analyse all the toxicity parameters before using as a drug delivery vessel or in cell imaging. It is also important to study the environmental nanotoxicity that could be possible by using graphene as a sensor in the detection of inorganic analytes and biological compounds. When combined, all this parameters’ points towards a long journey in the future of graphene research.

Besides providing a base of research in 2D materials the field of materials science and nanotechnology, graphene-based materials is an inspiration for the new classes of 2D materials [265]. Some of these new materials have even been able to outrun graphitic materials in some applications [266]. Some of the renowned example is like that of borophene which have been found to possess mechanical properties better than that of graphene[267]. Silicon-based 2D materials like silicene were found to have better electrical properties than graphene and rGO, and better tunability of the band structures[268]. In a very recent work graphene network like structure of molybdenum which has been named molybdenene has been reported. The microwave assisted synthesis of molybdenene was assisted by the use of graphene, which gave rise to the triclinic crystal structure [269]. It is expected that new materials will develop with progress in science. But under the current circumstances, based on the popularity and thousands of research works on graphene following the trend it is expected that graphene research would still play an active role for another decade, before getting any commercial aspect.

Sumon Santra: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Avigyan Bose: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ashadul Adalder: Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.
